Redis pub/sub is fire-and-forget — for guaranteed delivery, combine with a message queue
Presence tracking: SADD user to room set, SREM on disconnect, SMEMBERS to list online users
For message ordering, use server-assigned timestamps (not client clocks) as the source of truth

topics.title: SQL vs NoSQL
topics.description: SQL databases are relational, use structured schemas and SQL for queries. NoSQL databases are non-relational, offering flexible schemas and various data models. Choosing between them depends on data structure, scale, and consistency requirements.

youtubeVideos.title: When to Use SQL vs NoSQL

resources.title: SQL vs NoSQL - System Design Primer

interviewQuestions.q: When should you use SQL vs NoSQL?
interviewQuestions.a: Use SQL when: data is structured with clear relationships, you need ACID transactions, complex queries with JOINs are common, data integrity is critical (financial systems). Use NoSQL when: schema evolves rapidly, data is semi-structured or unstructured, you need horizontal scalability, high write throughput is required, or data access patterns are simple key-value or document lookups.

interviewQuestions.q: Can you use both SQL and NoSQL in the same system?
interviewQuestions.a: Yes, polyglot persistence is common. Use SQL for transactional data (orders, payments), NoSQL document store for product catalogs, Redis for caching/sessions, Elasticsearch for search, and a graph database for recommendations. Each database type excels at different access patterns.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the ACID properties and why do they matter?
interviewQuestions.a: Atomicity: transactions are all-or-nothing. Consistency: data moves from one valid state to another. Isolation: concurrent transactions don't interfere. Durability: committed data survives failures. ACID guarantees are crucial for financial systems, booking systems, and any application where partial updates would cause data corruption.

tips: Default to SQL unless you have a specific reason for NoSQL — relational databases are incredibly versatile and well-understood.
In interviews, justify your database choice based on access patterns, not just scale.

topics.title: Database Replication
topics.description: Database replication maintains copies of data across multiple servers for high availability, read scaling, and disaster recovery.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the types of database replication?
interviewQuestions.a: Synchronous: all replicas must confirm write before it is acknowledged (strong consistency, higher latency). Asynchronous: write is acknowledged immediately, replicas updated later (eventual consistency, lower latency). Semi-synchronous: at least one replica confirms (compromise). Statement-based: replays SQL statements. Row-based: replicates actual data changes. Logical: application-level replication.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you handle replication lag?
interviewQuestions.a: Monitor lag metrics, use read-your-own-writes consistency (route reads to primary after writes), use monotonic reads (ensure client reads from same replica), implement causal consistency where needed, set up alerts for excessive lag, and scale read replicas to reduce individual load.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the split-brain problem in replication?
interviewQuestions.a: Split-brain occurs when network partition causes both nodes to think they are the primary, accepting writes independently. This leads to data divergence. Prevention: use quorum-based systems (majority must agree), fencing mechanisms (STONITH), shared storage with locks, or external arbitrators (witness nodes).

tips: Always monitor replication lag — it is the #1 indicator of replication health.
Use read replicas for analytics and reporting queries to offload the primary.

topics.title: Database Sharding
topics.description: Sharding distributes data across multiple databases so each holds a subset. This enables horizontal write scaling beyond what a single server can handle.

interviewQuestions.q: What is database sharding and when do you need it?
interviewQuestions.a: Sharding splits data across multiple databases using a shard key. Each shard holds a subset of data. Need it when: a single database cannot handle write volume, data size exceeds single server storage, you need geographic data locality. Common shard keys: user ID, geographic region, tenant ID. Choose keys that distribute data evenly and align with query patterns.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the challenges of sharding?
interviewQuestions.a: Cross-shard queries are expensive (JOINs across shards), rebalancing data when adding shards is complex, maintaining referential integrity across shards, uneven data distribution (hotspots), increased operational complexity, and application must be shard-aware. Some operations become impossible or very expensive (global sorting, aggregation).

interviewQuestions.q: What sharding strategies exist?
interviewQuestions.a: Range-based: shard by key ranges (users A-M, N-Z) — easy to implement but can cause hotspots. Hash-based: hash the shard key (consistent hashing) — even distribution but range queries are hard. Directory-based: lookup table maps keys to shards — flexible but the directory is a bottleneck/SPOF. Geographic: shard by region — good for data locality requirements.

tips: Avoid sharding as long as possible — try vertical scaling, read replicas, caching, and query optimization first.
Choose a shard key that matches your most common query pattern and distributes data evenly.

topics.title: Database Federation
topics.description: Federation (functional partitioning) splits databases by function. For example, separate databases for users, products, and orders. Each database handles its own read and write traffic.

interviewQuestions.q: What is database federation?
interviewQuestions.a: Federation splits databases by function — a users database, products database, orders database, etc. Each functional database handles its own traffic independently. Benefits: smaller databases are faster, each can be scaled independently, failure isolation, and each team can own their database. Drawbacks: cross-function JOINs require application-level joins, and more complex infrastructure.

interviewQuestions.q: How does federation differ from sharding?
interviewQuestions.a: Federation splits by function (users DB, orders DB) — each database has different tables. Sharding splits by data (users 1-1M on shard 1, users 1M-2M on shard 2) — each shard has the same tables. Federation is simpler to implement but has limited scalability per function. Sharding is more complex but can scale each function further.

interviewQuestions.q: When should you use federation?
interviewQuestions.a: When different functional areas have different scaling needs, different teams own different domains (microservices), you want failure isolation between domains, and cross-domain queries are rare. It aligns naturally with Domain-Driven Design and microservices architecture where each bounded context owns its data.

tips: Federation aligns naturally with microservices — each service owns its database.
Combine federation with sharding: federate by function first, then shard individual databases that grow too large.

topics.title: Denormalization
topics.description: Denormalization adds redundant data to one or more tables to avoid expensive JOINs. It trades write complexity and storage for read performance.

interviewQuestions.q: What is denormalization and when should you use it?
interviewQuestions.a: Denormalization stores redundant copies of data to avoid JOINs at read time. Use it when: read performance is critical and JOINs are a bottleneck, your system is read-heavy (10:1 or higher read-to-write ratio), you are using a NoSQL database that does not support JOINs, or after federation/sharding makes cross-database JOINs impossible.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the risks of denormalization?
interviewQuestions.a: Data redundancy increases storage costs, write operations become more complex (must update all copies), risk of data inconsistency if updates miss a copy, harder to maintain data integrity, and schema changes require updating multiple locations. Mitigate with: materialized views, change data capture (CDC), or application-level consistency checks.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you keep denormalized data consistent?
interviewQuestions.a: Approaches: application-level updates (update all copies in a transaction), change data capture (CDC) to propagate changes asynchronously, materialized views that auto-refresh, event-driven updates via message queues, and periodic reconciliation jobs that detect and fix inconsistencies.

tips: Normalize first, then denormalize specific read paths that are proven bottlenecks — do not denormalize prematurely.
Use materialized views or CDC (Change Data Capture) to keep denormalized data in sync automatically.

topics.title: SQL Tuning
topics.description: SQL tuning involves optimizing queries, indexes, and database configuration for better performance. It is often the first step before considering more complex scaling strategies.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the key SQL tuning strategies?
interviewQuestions.a: 1) Add proper indexes (B-tree for equality/range, hash for equality, composite for multi-column), 2) Use EXPLAIN/ANALYZE to understand query plans, 3) Avoid SELECT * — fetch only needed columns, 4) Optimize JOINs (use indexed columns, prefer smaller result sets), 5) Use connection pooling, 6) Tune database configuration (buffer pool, query cache), 7) Partition large tables, 8) Use read replicas for analytics.

interviewQuestions.q: How do database indexes work?
interviewQuestions.a: Indexes are data structures (typically B-trees) that allow the database to find rows without scanning the entire table. A B-tree index maintains sorted data enabling O(log n) lookups. Composite indexes support queries on the leftmost prefix of columns. Indexes speed up reads but slow down writes (index must be updated). Too many indexes waste storage and slow INSERT/UPDATE operations.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the N+1 query problem?
interviewQuestions.a: N+1 occurs when code fetches a list of N items, then makes one additional query per item to fetch related data — resulting in N+1 total queries instead of 1-2. Fix with: eager loading (JOIN in one query), batch loading (WHERE id IN (...)), or GraphQL/DataLoader pattern. This is one of the most common performance issues in web applications.

tips: Always check EXPLAIN ANALYZE output before and after adding indexes — verify the optimizer actually uses them.
The N+1 query problem is the most common performance issue — always batch or eager-load related data.

topics.title: Relational Database Management Systems (RDBMS)
topics.description: RDBMS store data in tables with rows and columns, enforce schemas, support ACID transactions, and use SQL for querying. They are the foundation of most business applications.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the advantages of RDBMS?
interviewQuestions.a: ACID transactions for data integrity, mature and well-understood technology, powerful query language (SQL with JOINs, aggregations, subqueries), strong schema enforcement, rich ecosystem of tools and ORMs, excellent for complex relationships between entities, and decades of optimization research.

interviewQuestions.q: When would you not use an RDBMS?
interviewQuestions.a: When schema changes are very frequent, data is unstructured or semi-structured (logs, documents), you need massive write throughput that exceeds single-server capacity, data does not have clear relationships, you need extreme horizontal scalability, or you are working with graph-like data (social networks).

interviewQuestions.q: Compare PostgreSQL and MySQL.
interviewQuestions.a: PostgreSQL: more feature-rich (JSON support, window functions, CTEs, full-text search), better standard compliance, extensible (custom types, functions), better for complex queries. MySQL: simpler, faster for simple queries, better replication ecosystem, wider hosting support, InnoDB storage engine is reliable. Both are excellent — PostgreSQL is more versatile, MySQL is more straightforward.

tips: PostgreSQL is the best default choice for new projects — it has the richest feature set among open-source RDBMS.
Use connection pooling (PgBouncer, ProxySQL) — database connections are expensive to establish.

topics.title: Key-Value Store
topics.description: Key-value stores provide simple get/set operations with O(1) lookups. They are used for caching, session storage, and simple data models where the access pattern is always by key.

interviewQuestions.q: What are key-value stores and when should you use them?
interviewQuestions.a: Key-value stores map keys to values with O(1) lookups. Use for: caching (Redis, Memcached), session storage, user preferences, shopping carts, rate limiting, leaderboards, and any simple lookup-by-key pattern. They offer extreme performance and scalability but limited query capability — you can only look up by key, not by value attributes.

interviewQuestions.q: Compare Redis and Memcached.
interviewQuestions.a: Redis: supports data structures (strings, lists, sets, hashes, sorted sets), persistence options, pub/sub, Lua scripting, clustering, and replication. Memcached: simpler, multi-threaded (better for simple caching on multi-core), supports only string values, no persistence, simpler horizontal scaling. Choose Redis for feature-rich needs, Memcached for simple, high-throughput caching.

interviewQuestions.q: How does DynamoDB work as a key-value store?
interviewQuestions.a: DynamoDB is a managed key-value and document database. Tables have a partition key (hash) and optional sort key. Single-digit millisecond latency at any scale. Supports secondary indexes for alternative access patterns. Auto-scales throughput. Offers both eventual and strong consistency per-read. Pricing is per read/write capacity unit. Excellent for serverless and high-scale applications.

tips: Redis is the Swiss Army knife of databases — use it for caching, sessions, rate limiting, pub/sub, and queues.
Design your data model around access patterns when using key-value stores — you cannot query by value efficiently.

topics.title: Document Store
topics.description: Document stores organize data as flexible JSON/BSON documents. Each document can have a different structure. They excel at storing semi-structured data and enabling rapid development with evolving schemas.

interviewQuestions.q: What are document stores and when should you use them?
interviewQuestions.a: Document stores store data as JSON-like documents with flexible schemas. Use for: content management, product catalogs, user profiles, event logging, and any domain where entities have varying attributes. Documents can be nested, reducing the need for JOINs. Best when you read/write entire documents and do not need complex cross-document queries.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the limitations of document stores?
interviewQuestions.a: Complex JOINs across documents are expensive or unsupported, less efficient for highly relational data, potential for data duplication (embedded documents), weaker transaction support (though MongoDB now supports multi-document ACID transactions), and query optimization is less mature than RDBMS.

interviewQuestions.q: How does MongoDB handle scaling?
interviewQuestions.a: MongoDB uses sharding (distributing data across servers using a shard key), replica sets (primary + secondaries for HA), and automatic failover. The mongos router directs queries to the correct shard. Config servers store cluster metadata. It supports range-based and hash-based sharding strategies.

tips: Embed related data in a single document when they are always accessed together — this avoids JOINs and improves read performance.
Use references (like foreign keys) when related data is large, frequently updated independently, or accessed separately.

topics.title: Wide Column Store
topics.description: Wide column stores organize data by columns rather than rows. They excel at handling large volumes of data with high write throughput and are ideal for time-series data, IoT, and analytics.

resources.title: Wide Column Store - System Design Primer

interviewQuestions.q: What are wide column stores?
interviewQuestions.a: Wide column stores use tables, rows, and columns, but unlike RDBMS, columns can vary per row and are grouped into column families. Optimized for write-heavy workloads and reading specific columns. Examples: Cassandra, HBase, Google Bigtable, ScyllaDB. Used for time-series data, IoT sensor data, messaging systems, and recommendation engines.

interviewQuestions.q: How does Cassandra achieve high availability?
interviewQuestions.a: Cassandra uses a ring architecture with no single point of failure — all nodes are equal. Data is partitioned across nodes using consistent hashing. Replication factor determines how many copies exist. Tunable consistency (ONE, QUORUM, ALL) lets you balance consistency and availability per operation. Gossip protocol manages cluster membership and failure detection.

interviewQuestions.q: When should you choose a wide column store over RDBMS?
interviewQuestions.a: When you have massive write throughput requirements (millions of writes/second), time-series or event data, data that is naturally columnar, need for linear horizontal scalability, and can work with limited query patterns (primarily key-based lookups). Avoid for complex queries, transactions, or when strong consistency across all operations is required.

tips: Model data in Cassandra around your queries — design tables to serve specific query patterns, even if it means duplicating data.
Wide column stores are ideal for time-series data — partition by time period and sort within partitions.

topics.title: Graph Databases
topics.description: Graph databases store data as nodes and edges (relationships). They excel at traversing relationships and are ideal for social networks, recommendation engines, fraud detection, and knowledge graphs.

interviewQuestions.q: What are graph databases and when should you use them?
interviewQuestions.a: Graph databases model data as nodes (entities), edges (relationships), and properties. Use when: relationships are the core of your data model (social networks, recommendation engines), you need multi-hop traversals (friends of friends, shortest path), the number and type of relationships are dynamic, and relational JOINs become prohibitively expensive for relationship queries.

interviewQuestions.q: How do graph databases differ from relational databases for relationship queries?
interviewQuestions.a: In RDBMS, relationship queries require JOINs that become exponentially expensive with depth (friends-of-friends-of-friends). Graph databases store relationships as first-class citizens — traversing a relationship is O(1) regardless of total data size. A 6-degree connection query that takes minutes in SQL takes milliseconds in a graph database.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the use cases for graph databases?
interviewQuestions.a: Social networks (friend recommendations, mutual connections), fraud detection (finding suspicious transaction patterns), knowledge graphs (Wikipedia, Google Knowledge Graph), network topology (routing, dependency analysis), recommendation engines (users who bought X also bought Y), access control (permission graphs), and supply chain management.

tips: Use graph databases when your most important queries involve traversing relationships — not just storing them.
Graph databases complement rather than replace RDBMS — use both for different parts of your system.

topics.title: NoSQL
topics.description: NoSQL is an umbrella term for non-relational databases. They provide flexible schemas, horizontal scalability, and are optimized for specific data models and access patterns.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the main types of NoSQL databases?
interviewQuestions.a: 1) Key-Value: Redis, DynamoDB — simple lookups by key. 2) Document: MongoDB, CouchDB — flexible JSON documents. 3) Wide Column: Cassandra, HBase — column-family storage for massive data. 4) Graph: Neo4j, Neptune — relationship traversal. Each type is optimized for different access patterns and data models.

interviewQuestions.q: What is BASE and how does it differ from ACID?
interviewQuestions.a: BASE: Basically Available (system always responds), Soft state (state may change without input due to eventual consistency), Eventually consistent (data converges over time). ACID provides strong guarantees at the cost of performance/availability. BASE trades consistency for availability and performance — suitable for systems that can tolerate temporary inconsistency.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you choose the right NoSQL database?
interviewQuestions.a: Match the database to your access pattern: Key-value for simple lookups and caching. Document for flexible schemas and embedded data. Wide column for time-series and massive write throughput. Graph for relationship-heavy queries. Consider: consistency requirements, scalability needs, query complexity, operational maturity, and cloud provider managed options.

tips: NoSQL does not mean 'no SQL' — many NoSQL databases support SQL-like query languages (CQL for Cassandra, N1QL for Couchbase).
Choose your NoSQL database based on your primary access pattern — each type excels at one thing.

Experience the polyglot persistence pattern used by Netflix, Uber, and other large-scale systems.

Implement CRUD operations with proper transaction management

Add read replicas and measure replication lag

tasks.title: Design the data model

tasks.title: Implement relational CRUD

For CDC, consider Debezium or simple application-level events as a starting point

topics.title: Caching
topics.description: Caching stores frequently accessed data in a fast-access layer to reduce latency and backend load. It is one of the most effective performance optimization strategies.

interviewQuestions.q: What is caching and why is it important in system design?
interviewQuestions.a: Caching stores copies of frequently accessed data in a fast storage layer (memory) to reduce response time and backend load. A cache hit avoids expensive database queries or API calls. Caching is critical because: memory access is ~100x faster than SSD, 10000x faster than HDD, reduces database load, improves user experience, and enables systems to handle more traffic.

interviewQuestions.q: What are cache eviction policies?
interviewQuestions.a: LRU (Least Recently Used): evict the entry accessed longest ago — most common. LFU (Least Frequently Used): evict the entry used least often. FIFO (First In First Out): evict oldest entries. TTL (Time To Live): entries expire after a set duration. Random: randomly evict entries. LRU is the best default for most applications.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the cache stampede problem?
interviewQuestions.a: When a popular cache entry expires, many concurrent requests simultaneously hit the database to regenerate it, potentially overloading the system. Solutions: lock/mutex (only one request regenerates, others wait), probabilistic early expiration (randomly refresh before TTL), stale-while-revalidate (serve stale data while refreshing in background), and pre-warming (refresh before expiration).

tips: Cache the most expensive and frequently accessed data first — use the 80/20 rule.
Always have a cache invalidation strategy — stale data can be worse than slow data.

topics.title: Client Caching
topics.description: Client-side caching stores data on the user's device (browser cache, local storage, service workers). It eliminates network round trips entirely for cached content.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the types of client-side caching?
interviewQuestions.a: Browser HTTP cache (Cache-Control headers), localStorage/sessionStorage (manual key-value), IndexedDB (structured data), Service Workers (programmable network proxy cache), memory cache (in-app data structures), and mobile app caches. Each has different size limits, persistence, and invalidation strategies.

interviewQuestions.q: How do HTTP caching headers work?
interviewQuestions.a: Cache-Control: max-age=3600 (cache for 1 hour), no-cache (always revalidate), no-store (never cache), public/private (CDN caching), stale-while-revalidate (serve stale while fetching fresh). ETag: content fingerprint for conditional requests. Last-Modified: timestamp-based validation. Vary: cache by request headers (Accept-Encoding, etc.).

interviewQuestions.q: What is a Service Worker cache and when should you use it?
interviewQuestions.a: Service Workers are JavaScript workers that intercept network requests. They can cache responses and serve them offline. Use for: Progressive Web Apps (PWA), offline functionality, custom caching strategies (cache-first, network-first, stale-while-revalidate), and background sync. They give you full programmatic control over caching behavior.

tips: Use Cache-Control: immutable for versioned assets (style.abc123.css) to prevent unnecessary revalidation.
Combine short TTL for API responses with stale-while-revalidate for the best user experience.

topics.title: CDN Caching
topics.description: CDN caching stores content at edge locations worldwide, serving users from the nearest point of presence. It reduces latency for static content and can also cache API responses.

interviewQuestions.q: How does CDN caching work?
interviewQuestions.a: CDN edge servers cache content based on Cache-Control headers from the origin. On cache miss, the edge fetches from origin, caches the response, and serves it. Subsequent requests from nearby users hit the edge cache. CDNs use TTL, cache keys (URL + headers), and purge APIs for invalidation. Origin Shield adds a centralized cache between edge and origin to reduce origin load.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you maximize CDN cache hit ratio?
interviewQuestions.a: Use consistent cache keys (avoid unnecessary query parameters), set appropriate TTL, use versioned filenames for static assets, minimize Vary header values, implement Origin Shield, pre-warm cache for popular content, and use stale-while-revalidate. Monitor cache hit ratio — aim for 90%+ for static content.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you invalidate CDN cache?
interviewQuestions.a: TTL-based expiration (automatic), purge API (invalidate specific URLs or patterns), versioned URLs (best for static assets), tag-based purging (Fastly, Cloudflare), and deploy-time invalidation. Versioned URLs are the most reliable — no purge needed, and old versions remain cached until they naturally expire.

tips: Versioned filenames are better than cache purging — they guarantee users get the new version instantly.
Use Origin Shield to reduce origin load and improve cache consistency across edge locations.

topics.title: Web Server Caching
topics.description: Web server caching stores responses at the reverse proxy or web server level. Tools like NGINX and Varnish can cache responses to reduce application server load.

interviewQuestions.q: What is web server caching?
interviewQuestions.a: The web server (NGINX, Apache) or reverse proxy (Varnish) caches full HTTP responses. When the same request arrives, the cached response is returned without hitting the application server. This is especially effective for: API responses that do not change frequently, rendered HTML pages, and any GET request with stable output.

interviewQuestions.q: How does Varnish Cache work?
interviewQuestions.a: Varnish sits in front of web servers as an HTTP accelerator. It caches responses in memory based on URL, headers, and cookies. VCL (Varnish Configuration Language) provides fine-grained control over what is cached, cache duration, and purging. It can serve thousands of requests per second from cache, dramatically reducing application server load.

interviewQuestions.q: When should you use web server caching vs application-level caching?
interviewQuestions.a: Web server caching is best for: full page caching, static/semi-static content, reducing application server load. Application caching is better for: granular data caching (database query results), shared cache across requests (user sessions), and complex invalidation logic tied to business events.

tips: NGINX proxy_cache is easy to set up and can dramatically reduce application server load for read-heavy APIs.
Use microcaching (TTL of 1-5 seconds) for dynamic content — even very short caching handles traffic spikes.

topics.title: Database Caching
topics.description: Databases have built-in caching mechanisms — query cache, buffer pool, and connection caching. Understanding and tuning these can significantly improve performance without application changes.

interviewQuestions.q: What database-level caching mechanisms exist?
interviewQuestions.a: Buffer pool (InnoDB): caches data pages and index pages in memory — the most important cache. Query cache: caches full query results (deprecated in MySQL 8.0, use application-level caching instead). Connection pooling: caches database connections to avoid connection overhead. Prepared statement cache: caches query execution plans.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you size the database buffer pool?
interviewQuestions.a: Allocate 60-80% of available RAM to the buffer pool for dedicated database servers. Monitor the buffer pool hit ratio — should be 99%+. If hit ratio is low, increase buffer pool size or optimize queries to access less data. Use SHOW ENGINE INNODB STATUS in MySQL or pg_stat_bgwriter in PostgreSQL to monitor.

interviewQuestions.q: Why was MySQL query cache removed?
interviewQuestions.a: MySQL 8.0 removed the query cache because: it uses a global mutex (serializes all queries), any write to a table invalidates all cached queries for that table, the overhead of checking and maintaining the cache often exceeded its benefits, and it does not scale with multi-core CPUs. Application-level caching (Redis) is more effective and flexible.

tips: Size your database buffer pool to hold your working set in memory — this is the single most impactful database tuning parameter.
Use EXPLAIN ANALYZE to identify queries that scan too many rows — fixing these reduces the need for caching.

topics.title: Application Caching
topics.description: Application-level caching uses in-memory stores like Redis or Memcached to cache computed results, database query results, API responses, and session data.

interviewQuestions.q: What should you cache at the application level?
interviewQuestions.a: Database query results (most common), computed/aggregated data, external API responses, session data, configuration/feature flags, rendered templates/views, and expensive computations. Cache data that is: read frequently, expensive to compute, relatively stable, and tolerant of slight staleness.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you decide cache TTL?
interviewQuestions.a: Consider: how frequently does the data change, how critical is freshness, what is the cost of a cache miss, and what is acceptable staleness. High-frequency data (stock prices): seconds. Medium (product catalog): minutes to hours. Low-frequency (user profiles): hours to days. Use events or webhooks to invalidate early when data changes.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the two main caching patterns?
interviewQuestions.a: Cache-aside (lazy loading): application checks cache, on miss queries database and populates cache. Simple but risks cache stampede. Write-through: application writes to cache and database simultaneously. Cache is always fresh but adds write latency. Read-through and write-behind are database-driven variants of these patterns.

tips: Start with cache-aside pattern — it is the simplest and most widely used.
Always set a TTL even on write-through caches as a safety net against memory leaks and stale data.

topics.title: Cache Aside
topics.description: Cache-aside (lazy loading) is the most common caching pattern. The application checks the cache first, and on a miss, loads from the database and populates the cache.

interviewQuestions.q: How does cache-aside work?
interviewQuestions.a: 1) Application receives a read request. 2) Check the cache for the data. 3) Cache hit: return cached data. 4) Cache miss: query the database, store result in cache with TTL, return data. For writes: write to database, then invalidate (delete) the cache entry. The next read will populate the cache with fresh data.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the pros and cons of cache-aside?
interviewQuestions.a: Pros: only requested data is cached (efficient memory use), resilient to cache failures (falls back to database), simple implementation. Cons: cache miss penalty (extra round trip), cache stampede risk (many misses simultaneously), data can be stale until TTL expires or explicit invalidation.

interviewQuestions.q: Should you update or invalidate the cache on writes?
interviewQuestions.a: Prefer invalidation (delete the cache entry) over update. Reasons: updates can have race conditions (two concurrent writes may cache stale data), invalidation is simpler and safer, the next read will load fresh data, and you avoid caching data that might not be read again. Only use cache updates if cache misses are extremely expensive.

tips: Always invalidate (delete) cache entries on writes rather than updating them — it avoids race conditions.
Use TTL as a safety net even when you invalidate on writes — it protects against missed invalidations.

topics.title: Write-Through
topics.description: Write-through caching writes data to both the cache and database simultaneously. The cache is always up to date, but write latency increases.

interviewQuestions.q: How does write-through caching work?
interviewQuestions.a: Every write goes to both the cache and the database. The write is only acknowledged after both succeed. This ensures the cache always has the latest data. Reads always get fresh data from cache. Trade-off: higher write latency (two writes per operation) but zero cache misses for recently written data.

interviewQuestions.q: When should you use write-through?
interviewQuestions.a: When data freshness is critical, read-after-write consistency is required, the data is frequently read after being written, and you can tolerate slightly higher write latency. Common in: user profile updates (immediately visible), configuration changes, and data that is read much more frequently than written.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the disadvantages of write-through?
interviewQuestions.a: Higher write latency (two synchronous writes), most data written may never be read (wasted cache space), cache fills up with potentially unneeded data, and if the cache fails, you lose the performance benefit. Combine with TTL to evict data that is not being read.

tips: Write-through works best when combined with TTL to evict data that is written but rarely read.
Pair write-through with cache-aside reads for the best of both worlds.

topics.title: Write-Behind (Write-Back)
topics.description: Write-behind caching writes to the cache immediately and asynchronously flushes to the database. This provides the lowest write latency but risks data loss if the cache fails before flushing.

interviewQuestions.q: How does write-behind caching work?
interviewQuestions.a: 1) Application writes to cache only. 2) Write is acknowledged immediately (lowest latency). 3) Cache asynchronously writes to database in the background (batched or periodic flush). Benefits: very low write latency, batch writes reduce database load. Risks: data loss if cache fails before flushing, complexity of ensuring flush reliability.

interviewQuestions.q: When is write-behind appropriate?
interviewQuestions.a: When write latency is critical and some data loss is acceptable (analytics, logging, counters), when you want to batch writes to reduce database load, or when the database cannot handle the write throughput directly. Gaming leaderboards, view counters, and telemetry data are good candidates.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you mitigate data loss risk in write-behind?
interviewQuestions.a: Use persistent cache (Redis with AOF persistence), replicate the cache, implement write-ahead logging in the cache layer, use shorter flush intervals, monitor flush lag, and implement circuit breakers that fall back to synchronous writes if the database is unreachable for too long.

tips: Write-behind is great for high-write-throughput scenarios where batching can dramatically reduce database load.
Always use persistent cache (Redis with AOF) for write-behind to minimize data loss risk.

topics.title: Refresh-Ahead
topics.description: Refresh-ahead proactively refreshes cache entries before they expire, predicting which entries will be needed soon. This avoids cache miss latency for frequently accessed data.

interviewQuestions.q: How does refresh-ahead caching work?
interviewQuestions.a: The cache tracks access patterns and proactively refreshes entries that are likely to be requested soon, before they expire. When an entry reaches a certain percentage of its TTL (e.g., 80%), a background thread fetches fresh data from the database and updates the cache. This eliminates cache miss latency for popular entries.

interviewQuestions.q: When should you use refresh-ahead?
interviewQuestions.a: When cache miss latency is unacceptable for user experience, data access patterns are predictable, you have a relatively small set of hot keys, and the cost of unnecessary refreshes is acceptable. Common in: frequently accessed configuration data, popular product pages, and dashboards with regular access patterns.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the drawbacks of refresh-ahead?
interviewQuestions.a: Wastes resources refreshing entries that may not be requested, prediction accuracy varies, adds complexity, and background refresh threads consume resources. If predictions are wrong, you refresh unnecessary data while missing data that is actually needed. Works best when the hot set is small and predictable.

tips: Refresh-ahead works best when you have a small, predictable set of hot keys — do not use it for large, unpredictable workloads.
The stale-while-revalidate HTTP header is a simpler alternative to refresh-ahead for web content.

project.title: Build a Multi-Layer Cache System

objectives: Implement L1 (in-memory LRU) and L2 (Redis distributed) caches
Build cache-aside, write-through, and write-behind patterns
Handle cache invalidation with event-driven pub/sub
Protect against thundering herd (cache stampede) with distributed locks
Implement cache warming for predictable traffic patterns

tasks.title: Build L1 in-memory cache

tasks.description: Fall through from L1 miss to L2. Implement cache-aside pattern with DB fallback.

tasks.title: Implement write-through caching
tasks.description: On writes, update cache and database synchronously. Ensure consistency.

tasks.title: Add thundering herd protection
tasks.description: Use distributed locks (Redis SETNX) to ensure only one request populates the cache on a miss.

Cache-aside: check cache → miss → read DB → populate cache. Simple and widely used.
For thundering herd: lock the cache key, let one request fetch, others wait or get slightly stale data
Cache invalidation via pub/sub ensures all application instances stay consistent

topics.title: Message Queues
topics.description: Message queues decouple producers from consumers by buffering messages. Producers send messages without waiting for consumers. This enables asynchronous processing, load leveling, and fault tolerance.

interviewQuestions.q: What are message queues and when should you use them?
interviewQuestions.a: Message queues store messages from producers until consumers process them. Use when: you need to decouple services, handle traffic spikes (buffer during peaks), ensure reliable delivery (messages persist until acknowledged), process tasks asynchronously (email sending, image processing), and distribute work across multiple consumers. Examples: RabbitMQ, SQS, Redis Streams.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the difference between message queues and event streaming?
interviewQuestions.a: Message queues (RabbitMQ, SQS): messages are consumed and deleted, typically one consumer per message, point-to-point. Event streaming (Kafka): events are stored in an immutable log, multiple consumers can read the same events, messages are retained for a configurable period, and consumers track their own offset. Use queues for task distribution, streaming for event-driven architectures.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you ensure message delivery guarantees?
interviewQuestions.a: At-most-once: send and forget (fastest, may lose messages). At-least-once: retry until acknowledged (may cause duplicates — consumers must be idempotent). Exactly-once: hardest to achieve, requires idempotent consumers + transactional producers (Kafka supports this). Most systems use at-least-once with idempotent consumers as the practical sweet spot.

tips: At-least-once delivery with idempotent consumers is the practical sweet spot for most systems.
Use dead-letter queues for messages that fail processing repeatedly — do not lose them silently.

topics.title: Task Queues
topics.description: Task queues distribute work items across worker processes. They are used for background processing — tasks that are too slow or resource-intensive to handle in a web request.

interviewQuestions.q: What are task queues and how do they differ from message queues?
interviewQuestions.a: Task queues are specialized message queues for distributing work to worker processes. They typically include: task serialization, worker management, result tracking, retry policies, scheduled tasks, and priority queues. Message queues are the transport mechanism; task queues add workflow management on top. Examples: Celery (Python), Hangfire (.NET), Sidekiq (Ruby), BullMQ (Node.js).

interviewQuestions.q: What types of tasks should use a task queue?
interviewQuestions.a: Long-running operations (report generation, data export), resource-intensive work (image/video processing, ML inference), external API calls (email sending, payment processing), batch operations (bulk data import, ETL), and any task that would make a web request too slow (>500ms response time).

interviewQuestions.q: How do you handle task failures?
interviewQuestions.a: Implement retry with exponential backoff, set max retry limits, use dead-letter queues for permanently failed tasks, log failure details for debugging, send alerts on repeated failures, and make tasks idempotent so retries are safe. Monitor task success/failure rates and processing times.

tips: Make every task idempotent — tasks may be retried due to worker crashes, timeouts, or network issues.
Set appropriate timeouts for tasks — a hung task should not block the worker forever.

topics.title: Back Pressure
topics.description: Back pressure is a mechanism where a system signals upstream that it is overloaded and cannot accept more work. It prevents cascading failures and resource exhaustion in distributed systems.

interviewQuestions.q: What is back pressure and why is it important?
interviewQuestions.a: Back pressure is a feedback mechanism where a component signals that it cannot accept more input. Without back pressure, an overwhelmed component accumulates unbounded queues, eventually running out of memory and crashing. With back pressure, the producer slows down or rejects requests, maintaining system stability. It is essential for building resilient distributed systems.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you implement back pressure?
interviewQuestions.a: Bounded queues (reject when full), rate limiting (limit requests per second), load shedding (drop lower-priority requests), circuit breakers (stop sending requests to failing services), HTTP 429 status codes (Too Many Requests), TCP flow control (built-in back pressure), and reactive streams (explicit demand signaling). The key is having a finite capacity and a clear response when that capacity is exceeded.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the difference between back pressure and load shedding?
interviewQuestions.a: Back pressure signals the sender to slow down — the system pushes back on incoming load. Load shedding drops excess requests without signaling the sender — the system discards work it cannot handle. Back pressure is cooperative (sender adapts), load shedding is unilateral (receiver drops). Both protect the system; they are often used together.

tips: Always use bounded queues — unbounded queues are a ticking time bomb that will eventually exhaust memory.
Back pressure should propagate through the entire system — if one component is overloaded, upstream components should slow down.

topics.title: Idempotent Operations
topics.description: An idempotent operation produces the same result regardless of how many times it is executed. This is critical for reliability in distributed systems where retries and duplicate messages are common.

interviewQuestions.q: What is idempotency and why is it important?
interviewQuestions.a: An idempotent operation can be applied multiple times without changing the result beyond the first application. Example: setting a value to 5 is idempotent (do it 10 times, still 5). Incrementing by 1 is NOT idempotent (10 retries = +10). Idempotency is crucial because: network failures cause retries, message queues may deliver duplicates, users may double-click, and distributed systems need safe retry semantics.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you make APIs idempotent?
interviewQuestions.a: Use idempotency keys: client sends a unique ID with each request. Server checks if it has already processed that ID — if yes, return the cached result. Stripe uses this pattern for payment APIs. For REST: GET, PUT, DELETE are naturally idempotent. POST is not — use idempotency keys. Store processed IDs with results in a database with TTL for cleanup.

interviewQuestions.q: Which HTTP methods are idempotent?
interviewQuestions.a: GET: always idempotent (read-only). PUT: idempotent (setting a value). DELETE: idempotent (deleting already-deleted resource is a no-op). POST: NOT idempotent (creates new resources). PATCH: NOT necessarily idempotent (depends on implementation). HEAD, OPTIONS: idempotent (read-only). Make POST idempotent using idempotency keys for critical operations like payments.

tips: Always make payment and critical business operations idempotent — double charges are unacceptable.
Use the Stripe pattern: require an Idempotency-Key header for POST requests to critical endpoints.

topics.title: HTTP
topics.description: HTTP (Hypertext Transfer Protocol) is the foundation of data communication on the web. Understanding HTTP methods, status codes, headers, and versions is essential for system design.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the key differences between HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, and HTTP/3?
interviewQuestions.a: HTTP/1.1: text-based, one request per connection (head-of-line blocking), connection keep-alive. HTTP/2: binary protocol, multiplexing (multiple requests over one connection), header compression (HPACK), server push. HTTP/3: uses QUIC (UDP-based), eliminates TCP head-of-line blocking, faster connection setup (0-RTT), better mobile performance.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the common HTTP status codes in system design?
interviewQuestions.a: 200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content, 301 Moved Permanently, 304 Not Modified, 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found, 409 Conflict, 429 Too Many Requests, 500 Internal Server Error, 502 Bad Gateway, 503 Service Unavailable, 504 Gateway Timeout. Know these for API design discussions.

interviewQuestions.q: What is HTTP keep-alive and why does it matter?
interviewQuestions.a: Keep-alive reuses TCP connections for multiple HTTP requests instead of opening a new connection per request. This eliminates TCP handshake overhead (1 RTT) and TLS handshake overhead (1-2 RTTs). Essential for performance — without keep-alive, each request incurs 50-150ms of connection setup. HTTP/1.1 defaults to keep-alive; HTTP/2 uses a single multiplexed connection.

tips: Use HTTP/2 for all modern web services — multiplexing alone provides significant performance improvement.
Return appropriate status codes — 429 for rate limiting, 503 for overload, 504 for upstream timeouts.

topics.title: TCP
topics.description: TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) provides reliable, ordered delivery of data between applications. It is the foundation for HTTP, database connections, and most internet communication.

interviewQuestions.q: How does TCP ensure reliable delivery?
interviewQuestions.a: TCP uses: three-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) for connection establishment, sequence numbers to order packets, acknowledgments (ACKs) to confirm receipt, retransmission of lost packets, flow control (sliding window), and congestion control (slow start, congestion avoidance). This guarantees ordered, complete delivery at the cost of some latency overhead.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the TCP three-way handshake?
interviewQuestions.a: 1) Client sends SYN (synchronize) with initial sequence number. 2) Server responds with SYN-ACK (acknowledge + its own SYN). 3) Client sends ACK. After this, data can flow. This takes 1 round trip time (RTT). TLS adds 1-2 more RTTs. TCP Fast Open (TFO) allows data in the SYN packet for repeat connections, reducing latency.

interviewQuestions.q: What is TCP head-of-line blocking?
interviewQuestions.a: If one packet is lost, TCP holds all subsequent packets until the lost one is retransmitted and received — even though they may be for different requests. This is a problem for HTTP/2 where multiple streams share one TCP connection. HTTP/3 solves this by using QUIC (UDP-based), where packet loss only blocks the affected stream.

tips: TCP is the right choice when reliability and ordering matter — HTTP, database connections, file transfers.
Optimize TCP for latency: enable TCP Fast Open, tune window sizes, and reduce connection setup overhead with keep-alive.

topics.title: UDP
topics.description: UDP (User Datagram Protocol) provides fast, connectionless communication without reliability guarantees. It is ideal for real-time applications where speed matters more than perfect delivery.

interviewQuestions.q: When should you use UDP over TCP?
interviewQuestions.a: Use UDP when: low latency is more important than reliability (gaming, VoIP, live streaming), you can tolerate some data loss, you need broadcast/multicast, or you want to implement custom reliability (QUIC). UDP has no connection setup overhead, no head-of-line blocking, and lower per-packet overhead.

interviewQuestions.q: What is QUIC and how does it relate to UDP?
interviewQuestions.a: QUIC is a transport protocol built on UDP by Google, now standardized as HTTP/3. It provides TCP-like reliability per stream (no head-of-line blocking across streams), built-in TLS 1.3 (0-RTT connection establishment), connection migration (survives IP changes — mobile networks), and multiplexed streams. QUIC gives you reliability where you want it without TCP's limitations.

interviewQuestions.q: Compare TCP and UDP for different use cases.
interviewQuestions.a: TCP: web browsing, APIs, file transfer, email, database connections — where reliability and ordering are essential. UDP: online gaming (real-time position updates), VoIP (voice calls), live video streaming, DNS queries, IoT sensor data, and QUIC/HTTP/3. The trend is toward UDP-based protocols with selective reliability (QUIC).

tips: UDP is not unreliable — it just gives you control over which reliability features you need (QUIC proves this).
For modern real-time applications, consider WebRTC (UDP-based) for peer-to-peer and QUIC for client-server.

topics.title: RPC (Remote Procedure Call)
topics.description: RPC allows a program to execute a procedure on a remote server as if it were a local function call. It abstracts network communication, making distributed systems easier to program.

interviewQuestions.q: What is RPC and how does it work?
interviewQuestions.a: RPC (Remote Procedure Call) makes calling a function on a remote server look like a local function call. The client calls a stub function, which serializes the parameters, sends them over the network, the server deserializes, executes the function, and returns the result. Examples: gRPC, Apache Thrift, XML-RPC. RPC hides network complexity but can create tight coupling.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the drawbacks of RPC?
interviewQuestions.a: Network failures are not the same as local failures (latency, partial failures), tight coupling between client and server, versioning can be challenging, debugging is harder (distributed calls), and the abstraction can be leaky (developers forget about network latency). Modern RPC frameworks (gRPC) address many of these with built-in timeouts, retries, and load balancing.

interviewQuestions.q: When should you use RPC vs REST?
interviewQuestions.a: Use RPC (gRPC) for: internal microservice communication, high-performance requirements, strong typing with code generation, streaming data, and polyglot environments. Use REST for: public APIs, browser clients, simplicity and wide compatibility, HATEOAS/discoverability, and when human readability matters.

tips: Use gRPC for internal microservice communication — it is faster and provides strong typing through Protocol Buffers.
Always set timeouts on RPC calls — a hanging call can cascade failures through your system.

topics.title: REST
topics.description: REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style for designing networked applications. RESTful APIs use HTTP methods to perform CRUD operations on resources identified by URLs.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the principles of REST?
interviewQuestions.a: 1) Client-Server separation, 2) Statelessness (no session state on server), 3) Cacheability (responses indicate if cacheable), 4) Uniform interface (standard HTTP methods on resources), 5) Layered system (client does not know if connected directly to server), 6) Code on demand (optional — server can send executable code). Resources are identified by URLs, manipulated via HTTP methods.

interviewQuestions.q: What makes a good REST API design?
interviewQuestions.a: Use nouns for resources (/users, /orders), HTTP methods for actions (GET read, POST create, PUT update, DELETE remove), proper status codes, pagination for collections, versioning (URL or header), consistent naming (kebab-case, plural nouns), HATEOAS links, filtering/sorting via query parameters, and meaningful error responses with error codes and messages.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the disadvantages of REST?
interviewQuestions.a: Over-fetching (getting more data than needed), under-fetching (needing multiple requests for related data), no built-in real-time support (need WebSocket/SSE), less efficient than binary protocols (JSON text overhead), and chatty for complex operations. GraphQL and gRPC address some of these limitations.

tips: Design REST APIs around resources (nouns), not actions (verbs) — /orders not /createOrder.
Always version your API from day one — breaking changes without versioning are the #1 API design mistake.

topics.title: gRPC
topics.description: gRPC is a high-performance RPC framework using Protocol Buffers for serialization and HTTP/2 for transport. It provides strongly typed APIs, bidirectional streaming, and code generation.

interviewQuestions.q: What is gRPC and when should you use it?
interviewQuestions.a: gRPC is Google's RPC framework using Protocol Buffers (binary serialization) over HTTP/2. Use for: microservice-to-microservice communication, real-time streaming (bidirectional), polyglot services (code generation in 10+ languages), low-latency internal APIs, and mobile clients (efficient binary format). Not ideal for: browser clients (limited support), public APIs (REST is more universal).

interviewQuestions.q: What are the four gRPC communication patterns?
interviewQuestions.a: 1) Unary: single request, single response (like REST). 2) Server streaming: single request, stream of responses (real-time updates). 3) Client streaming: stream of requests, single response (uploading). 4) Bidirectional streaming: both sides stream simultaneously (chat, gaming). These patterns make gRPC more versatile than REST for real-time communication.

interviewQuestions.q: How does Protocol Buffers compare to JSON?
interviewQuestions.a: Protobuf: binary format (3-10x smaller), strongly typed with schema (.proto files), faster serialization/deserialization (5-10x), code generation for multiple languages, schema evolution with backward compatibility. JSON: human-readable, no schema required, universal support, easier debugging. Use Protobuf for internal services (performance), JSON for public APIs (accessibility).

tips: Use gRPC for internal microservice communication and REST for public-facing APIs — this is the most common pattern.
Define .proto files as your API contract — they serve as both documentation and code generation source.

topics.title: GraphQL
topics.description: GraphQL is a query language for APIs that lets clients request exactly the data they need. It solves REST's over-fetching and under-fetching problems with a single flexible endpoint.

interviewQuestions.q: What is GraphQL and when should you use it?
interviewQuestions.a: GraphQL is a query language where clients specify exactly what data they need in a single request. The server returns only the requested fields. Use when: clients have diverse data needs (mobile vs web), you want to reduce over-fetching/under-fetching, combining data from multiple services (BFF pattern), and rapid frontend development. Popular implementations: Apollo, Relay.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the challenges of GraphQL?
interviewQuestions.a: N+1 query problem (DataLoader solves this), complex authorization (field-level permissions), caching is harder (single endpoint, varying queries), query complexity attacks (malicious deep queries — use depth limiting), harder to rate limit (one endpoint), and learning curve for the team. REST is simpler for straightforward CRUD APIs.

interviewQuestions.q: How does GraphQL compare to REST and gRPC?
interviewQuestions.a: GraphQL: flexible queries, single endpoint, great for frontend-driven development. REST: simple, cacheable, widely supported, good for CRUD. gRPC: highest performance, binary format, streaming, great for internal services. Choose based on your primary constraint: flexibility (GraphQL), simplicity (REST), or performance (gRPC).

tips: Use GraphQL as a BFF (Backend for Frontend) layer that aggregates data from multiple microservices.
Always implement query depth limiting and complexity analysis to prevent abuse.

tasks.description: If a backend fails repeatedly, short-circuit requests and return fallback responses.

architectureHints: The gateway should be stateless — all state (sessions, rate limits) goes in Redis
Use middleware chaining: auth → rate limit → route → transform → respond
For response aggregation, fire parallel requests with Promise.all/asyncio.gather — don't serialize
WebSocket proxying requires handling the HTTP Upgrade handshake correctly

topics.title: Busy Database
topics.description: The Busy Database antipattern occurs when too much processing is offloaded to the database server, such as formatting, calculations, or business logic that should run in the application tier.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the Busy Database antipattern?
interviewQuestions.a: It occurs when the database handles processing that should happen in the application tier — data formatting, complex calculations, string manipulation, or business logic in stored procedures. This wastes expensive database CPU/memory resources, reduces database throughput, increases latency for all queries, and makes the database the bottleneck.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you fix the Busy Database antipattern?
interviewQuestions.a: Move processing to the application layer: formatting, sorting, filtering in code, not SQL. Use caching to reduce database load. Denormalize read-heavy data. Use materialized views for complex aggregations. Offload analytics to a read replica or data warehouse. Reserve the database for what it does best: storing, retrieving, and joining data.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you identify if your database is too busy?
interviewQuestions.a: Monitor: CPU utilization consistently above 70%, high query execution times, growing query queue, lock contention, replication lag increasing, slow query log filling up, and application timeouts on database calls. Use database profiling tools (pg_stat_statements, MySQL Performance Schema) to find the heaviest queries.

tips: The database should store and retrieve data — move all formatting, calculations, and business logic to the application layer.
Use EXPLAIN ANALYZE to identify expensive queries and optimize or move them to the application tier.

topics.title: Busy Frontend
topics.description: The Busy Frontend antipattern occurs when too much work runs on the client side, causing poor user experience with slow rendering, high memory usage, and unresponsive UI.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the Busy Frontend antipattern?
interviewQuestions.a: It occurs when the frontend performs expensive operations: heavy JavaScript computation, excessive DOM manipulation, large data processing, complex rendering loops, or real-time calculations that should happen server-side. Symptoms: high CPU usage in browser, janky scrolling, slow page interactions, poor Core Web Vitals scores, and excessive memory consumption.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you fix the Busy Frontend antipattern?
interviewQuestions.a: Move expensive computations to the server or Web Workers. Use virtualization for large lists (react-virtualized). Implement pagination instead of loading all data. Lazy load components and images. Use memoization to avoid redundant calculations. Debounce/throttle event handlers. Optimize rendering with proper keys and shouldComponentUpdate.

interviewQuestions.q: What are Core Web Vitals and why do they matter?
interviewQuestions.a: LCP (Largest Contentful Paint): loading performance — should be under 2.5s. FID (First Input Delay)/INP (Interaction to Next Paint): interactivity — should respond within 200ms. CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift): visual stability — should be under 0.1. Google uses these as ranking factors, and they directly impact user experience and conversion rates.

tips: Use Web Workers for CPU-intensive tasks — they run on a separate thread without blocking the UI.
Virtualize long lists — rendering 10,000 DOM nodes kills performance; virtualization renders only visible items.

topics.title: Chatty I/O
topics.description: The Chatty I/O antipattern occurs when an application makes many small I/O requests instead of fewer larger ones, causing excessive network overhead and latency.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the Chatty I/O antipattern?
interviewQuestions.a: Making many small network requests instead of fewer larger ones. Examples: N+1 query problem (fetch list, then query each item), calling an API for each item in a loop, reading file metadata one attribute at a time, and making separate database calls for related data. Each request adds network latency (1-100ms), connection overhead, and serialization cost.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you fix Chatty I/O?
interviewQuestions.a: Batch requests (fetch multiple items in one call), use JOINs instead of separate queries, implement DataLoader pattern (batch + deduplicate), use bulk APIs (POST /items/batch), cache frequently accessed data, denormalize to avoid secondary lookups, and use GraphQL to fetch all needed data in one request.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the N+1 query problem?
interviewQuestions.a: Fetching a list of N items (1 query), then making N additional queries to fetch related data for each item. Example: fetch 100 orders (1 query) then fetch customer for each order (100 queries) = 101 queries. Fix: use eager loading (JOIN), batch loading (WHERE id IN (...)), or DataLoader. This is the most common cause of Chatty I/O.

tips: Always batch database queries — use WHERE id IN (...) instead of looping with individual queries.
The DataLoader pattern (batch + deduplicate within a request) is essential for GraphQL and any data-fetching layer.

topics.title: Extraneous Fetching
topics.description: The Extraneous Fetching antipattern retrieves more data than needed, wasting bandwidth, memory, and processing time on data that is never used.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the Extraneous Fetching antipattern?
interviewQuestions.a: Retrieving more data than the application needs. Examples: SELECT * when only 3 columns are needed, fetching all user fields when only name is displayed, loading entire collections without pagination, and returning nested objects that clients ignore. This wastes database I/O, network bandwidth, memory, and CPU for serialization.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you fix Extraneous Fetching?
interviewQuestions.a: Select only needed columns (SELECT name, email FROM users), implement pagination (LIMIT/OFFSET or cursor-based), use field selection (GraphQL, sparse fieldsets in REST), implement API projections (?fields=name,email), lazy load relationships, and use DTOs/view models that contain only what the client needs.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the difference between over-fetching and under-fetching?
interviewQuestions.a: Over-fetching: getting more data than needed (REST returns full object when client needs 2 fields). Under-fetching: not getting enough data, requiring additional requests (REST returns user without address, client must fetch separately). GraphQL solves both — client specifies exactly what fields and relationships to return.

tips: Never use SELECT * in production code — always specify the columns you need.
Implement cursor-based pagination for large datasets — OFFSET pagination degrades at high page numbers.

topics.title: Improper Instantiation
topics.description: The Improper Instantiation antipattern creates expensive objects repeatedly instead of reusing them, wasting resources on object creation, connection establishment, and garbage collection.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the Improper Instantiation antipattern?
interviewQuestions.a: Creating new instances of expensive resources for each operation instead of reusing them. Common examples: creating a new HttpClient per request (socket exhaustion), opening a new database connection per query (connection limit exhaustion), instantiating new encryption objects per operation, and creating new thread pools per request. These objects have expensive setup costs that should be amortized.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you fix Improper Instantiation?
interviewQuestions.a: Use object pooling (connection pools, thread pools), singletons for stateless services (HttpClient, database clients), dependency injection with proper lifetimes (singleton, scoped, transient), IHttpClientFactory in .NET, and connection pooling libraries (HikariCP, PgBouncer). The key principle: create expensive objects once, reuse them many times.

interviewQuestions.q: Why is creating HttpClient per request problematic?
interviewQuestions.a: Each HttpClient holds a socket connection. Creating and disposing rapidly leads to socket exhaustion (TIME_WAIT state — ports not released immediately). The OS has a limited number of ports (~65K). Under load, you run out of ports and new connections fail. Solution: use a single static HttpClient instance or IHttpClientFactory for proper connection management.

tips: In .NET, always use IHttpClientFactory — never create HttpClient instances manually in request handlers.
Use connection pooling for database connections — PgBouncer for PostgreSQL, HikariCP for Java.

topics.title: Monolithic Persistence
topics.description: The Monolithic Persistence antipattern uses a single database for all data types and access patterns, even when different data would benefit from different storage technologies.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the Monolithic Persistence antipattern?
interviewQuestions.a: Using a single database for all data storage needs — relational data, documents, cache, search, time-series, and graphs all in one database. This leads to: suboptimal performance (one database cannot excel at everything), scaling limitations, single point of failure, and over-engineering the schema to fit different data models into one system.

interviewQuestions.q: What is polyglot persistence?
interviewQuestions.a: Using different database technologies for different data needs: RDBMS for transactional data, Redis for caching, Elasticsearch for search, MongoDB for documents, Cassandra for time-series, and Neo4j for graphs. Each database is optimized for its specific access pattern. Trade-off: more operational complexity but better performance and scalability per use case.

interviewQuestions.q: When is a single database acceptable?
interviewQuestions.a: For small applications, MVPs, and early-stage startups where operational simplicity is more important than optimal performance. A well-tuned PostgreSQL can handle relational data, JSON documents (jsonb), full-text search, and even key-value patterns. Start simple and adopt polyglot persistence as specific bottlenecks emerge.

tips: Start with PostgreSQL (handles many patterns well), then introduce specialized databases as bottlenecks emerge.
In microservices, each service should own its database — this naturally leads to polyglot persistence.

topics.title: No Caching
topics.description: The No Caching antipattern repeatedly fetches the same data from slow sources without storing it for reuse, causing unnecessary latency and load on backend systems.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the No Caching antipattern?
interviewQuestions.a: Repeatedly querying the database or calling external APIs for data that rarely changes, without any caching layer. Every request pays the full cost of data retrieval. This causes: unnecessary database load, higher latency, increased costs (API call charges), and poor scalability. Even short-lived caching (seconds to minutes) can dramatically improve performance.

interviewQuestions.q: What data should you cache?
interviewQuestions.a: Data that is: read frequently, expensive to compute or fetch, relatively stable (does not change every request), and tolerant of brief staleness. Examples: user profiles, product catalogs, configuration data, API responses, rendered templates, session data, and computed aggregations. Do NOT cache: real-time financial data, frequently changing state, or highly personalized per-request data.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you introduce caching to an existing system?
interviewQuestions.a: 1) Profile to find the most expensive/frequent operations. 2) Start with cache-aside pattern (simplest). 3) Add caching at the highest level possible (HTTP cache headers, CDN, then application cache, then database cache). 4) Set conservative TTLs initially and tune based on hit rates. 5) Monitor cache hit ratio — aim for 80%+ for frequently accessed data.

tips: Even caching for 1 second (microcaching) can handle massive traffic spikes without hitting the database.
Cache at every level: browser (Cache-Control), CDN (edge), reverse proxy (NGINX), application (Redis), database (buffer pool).

topics.title: Noisy Neighbor
topics.description: The Noisy Neighbor antipattern occurs when one tenant or component monopolizes shared resources, degrading performance for other tenants or components sharing the same infrastructure.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the Noisy Neighbor problem?
interviewQuestions.a: In shared infrastructure, one tenant or workload consumes disproportionate resources (CPU, memory, I/O, network), degrading performance for others. Common in: multi-tenant SaaS applications, shared databases, cloud VMs on the same physical host, Kubernetes pods on the same node, and shared message queues.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you mitigate the Noisy Neighbor problem?
interviewQuestions.a: Resource quotas and limits (Kubernetes resource limits, database connection limits), rate limiting per tenant, separate resource pools for high-value or high-usage tenants, dedicated infrastructure for critical workloads, queue prioritization, fair scheduling algorithms, and monitoring per-tenant resource consumption with auto-throttling.

interviewQuestions.q: How does Kubernetes handle the Noisy Neighbor problem?
interviewQuestions.a: Resource requests (guaranteed minimum CPU/memory), resource limits (maximum allowed), LimitRanges (default limits per namespace), ResourceQuotas (total limits per namespace), pod priority and preemption, node affinity/taints to isolate workloads, and quality of service classes (Guaranteed, Burstable, BestEffort).

tips: Always set resource limits in Kubernetes — a pod without limits can starve other pods on the same node.
For SaaS applications, implement per-tenant rate limiting and resource quotas from day one.

topics.title: Retry Storm
topics.description: A Retry Storm occurs when many clients simultaneously retry failed requests, amplifying the load on an already struggling system and potentially causing a cascading failure.

interviewQuestions.q: What is a retry storm?
interviewQuestions.a: When a service fails, all clients retry simultaneously with the same backoff, creating a synchronized burst of requests that hits the recovering service, causing it to fail again. This creates a cycle: failure → mass retries → more failure → more retries. The retry load can be 2-10x the normal load, making recovery impossible.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you prevent retry storms?
interviewQuestions.a: Exponential backoff with jitter (randomize retry timing to desynchronize clients), circuit breakers (stop retrying when failure rate is high), retry budgets (limit total retries across all clients), request deadlines (propagate timeouts), load shedding (reject excess requests quickly), and graceful degradation (serve cached/partial responses).

interviewQuestions.q: What is exponential backoff with jitter?
interviewQuestions.a: Instead of retrying at fixed intervals (1s, 1s, 1s), wait exponentially longer (1s, 2s, 4s, 8s). Add random jitter (+/- random offset) so clients do not all retry at the same time. Full jitter: random(0, base * 2^attempt). This spreads retries over time, reducing peak load on the recovering service. AWS recommends full jitter for best results.

tips: Always use exponential backoff WITH jitter — backoff without jitter still creates synchronized retry bursts.
Implement circuit breakers to fail fast when a downstream service is down — do not pile on retries.

topics.title: Synchronous I/O
topics.description: The Synchronous I/O antipattern blocks the calling thread while waiting for I/O operations to complete, wasting resources and limiting throughput.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the Synchronous I/O antipattern?
interviewQuestions.a: Blocking the calling thread while waiting for I/O operations (database queries, HTTP calls, file reads) to complete. The thread sits idle, consuming memory and OS resources, while waiting for the network or disk. In a thread-per-request server, this limits concurrency to the number of threads (typically hundreds). Async I/O allows thousands of concurrent operations.

interviewQuestions.q: How does async I/O improve performance?
interviewQuestions.a: Async I/O frees the thread during I/O waits, allowing it to handle other requests. A single thread can manage thousands of concurrent I/O operations. Node.js uses a single-threaded event loop with async I/O to handle massive concurrency. .NET uses async/await with the thread pool. This dramatically improves throughput for I/O-bound workloads.

interviewQuestions.q: When is synchronous I/O acceptable?
interviewQuestions.a: For CPU-bound operations (the thread is actually doing work, not waiting), simple scripts and tools, operations that must complete before proceeding (initialization), and when the simplicity benefit outweighs the performance cost (low-traffic internal tools). For any web server or API handling concurrent requests, async I/O is essential.

tips: In .NET, use async/await for ALL I/O operations — database calls, HTTP requests, file I/O.
Never use .Result or .Wait() on async methods in ASP.NET — it causes deadlocks and thread pool starvation.

project.title: Refactor a Deliberately Broken Service

objectives: Identify and fix N+1 query problems with eager loading
Eliminate chatty API calls with response aggregation and batching
Add pagination and result limiting to prevent unbounded queries
Convert synchronous blocking I/O to async operations
Add database indexes and measure query performance improvement

tasks.title: Profile the broken service
tasks.description: Run the service and identify bottlenecks using logging, query analysis, and response times.

tasks.title: Fix N+1 query problems
tasks.description: Replace loops of individual queries with batch/eager loading. Measure query count before/after.

tasks.title: Add pagination everywhere
tasks.description: All list endpoints return paginated results. Add cursor-based pagination for large datasets.

tasks.title: Optimize API chattiness
tasks.description: Combine multiple sequential API calls into batch endpoints. Reduce round trips.

tasks.title: Add database indexes
tasks.description: Identify slow queries with EXPLAIN ANALYZE. Add appropriate indexes. Measure improvement.

tasks.title: Fix memory leaks
