N+1 fix: use JOIN or IN (...) queries instead of one query per item in a loop
For pagination, cursor-based (WHERE id > last_seen_id) beats offset-based at scale
Profile memory usage over time — leaks show as a steadily increasing RSS
techStack: k6 or Artillery for load testing

topics.title: Health Monitoring
topics.description: Health monitoring verifies that the system and its components are functioning correctly. Health checks detect failures early, enable automated recovery, and provide visibility into system status.

interviewQuestions.q: What types of health checks should a system implement?
interviewQuestions.a: Liveness: is the process running? (restart if not). Readiness: can the service handle requests? (remove from LB if not). Startup: has the service finished initializing? (wait before checking liveness). Deep/dependency health: can the service reach its dependencies (DB, cache, APIs)? Shallow health checks are fast; deep checks verify end-to-end functionality.

interviewQuestions.q: How do health checks enable automated recovery?
interviewQuestions.a: Load balancers remove unhealthy instances from the pool. Kubernetes restarts pods that fail liveness probes. Auto-scaling replaces terminated instances. Circuit breakers trip when downstream health checks fail. DNS failover redirects traffic when health checks detect regional outage. All of this happens automatically, reducing MTTR (Mean Time To Recovery).

interviewQuestions.q: What should a health check endpoint return?
interviewQuestions.a: HTTP 200 with JSON body containing: overall status (healthy/degraded/unhealthy), individual dependency statuses, version information, and uptime. Do NOT expose sensitive internal details. Example: {"status": "healthy", "db": "ok", "cache": "ok", "uptime": "4d 12h"}. Use 503 for unhealthy status to enable automated tooling.

tips: Implement both shallow (fast, frequent) and deep (comprehensive, less frequent) health checks.
Never expose sensitive system details in health check responses — they should be available to load balancers and monitoring tools.

topics.title: Availability Monitoring
topics.description: Availability monitoring tracks whether services are accessible and responsive to users. It measures uptime, detects outages, and triggers alerts when services become unavailable.

interviewQuestions.q: What is availability monitoring?
interviewQuestions.a: Tracking whether services are reachable and responsive from the user's perspective. Includes: synthetic monitoring (simulated user requests from multiple locations), real user monitoring (RUM — tracking actual user experience), endpoint monitoring (periodic HTTP checks), and SSL certificate monitoring. Measures uptime percentage against SLA targets.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the difference between synthetic and real user monitoring?
interviewQuestions.a: Synthetic monitoring: scripted tests run from multiple locations at regular intervals. Detects outages proactively (before users notice). Consistent and predictable. Real user monitoring (RUM): captures actual user experience data from browsers/apps. Shows real performance, geographic distribution, and device-specific issues. Use both for comprehensive coverage.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you set up effective availability alerts?
interviewQuestions.a: Define SLIs (what to measure: error rate, latency, uptime), set SLOs (targets: 99.9% availability, p99 latency <500ms), configure alerts when SLO is at risk (error budget burn rate), use multi-window alerting (avoid false positives from brief spikes), route alerts to the right team, and include runbooks for common issues.

tips: Monitor from outside your infrastructure — synthetic checks from multiple regions detect issues your internal monitoring might miss.
Use error budgets (SRE approach) to balance reliability with feature velocity.

topics.title: Performance Monitoring
topics.description: Performance monitoring tracks response times, throughput, resource utilization, and identifies bottlenecks. It enables proactive optimization and capacity planning.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the key performance metrics to monitor?
interviewQuestions.a: Latency: p50, p95, p99 response times (not just averages). Throughput: requests per second (RPS). Error rate: percentage of failed requests. Saturation: how full are your resources (CPU, memory, disk, connections). These are the four golden signals defined by Google SRE. Additionally track: database query times, cache hit rates, and external API latencies.

interviewQuestions.q: What is distributed tracing and why is it important?
interviewQuestions.a: Distributed tracing tracks a request across multiple services, showing how it flows through the system with timing for each component. Tools: Jaeger, Zipkin, AWS X-Ray, Datadog APM. It answers: which service is causing latency? Where did the error originate? What is the critical path? Essential for debugging microservices where a single user request touches 5-20 services.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the difference between monitoring, logging, and tracing?
interviewQuestions.a: Monitoring: aggregated metrics over time (CPU usage, request count, error rate) — answers 'what is happening?' Logging: individual event records with details — answers 'what happened?' Tracing: request flow across services — answers 'where is the bottleneck?' Together they form the three pillars of observability. OpenTelemetry unifies all three.

tips: Always use percentiles (p95, p99) for latency, not averages — averages hide the worst-case experience.
Implement the four golden signals (latency, traffic, errors, saturation) as the foundation of your monitoring.

topics.title: Security Monitoring
topics.description: Security monitoring detects threats, unauthorized access, and suspicious activity. It is essential for protecting systems and responding to security incidents quickly.

interviewQuestions.q: What should security monitoring track?
interviewQuestions.a: Authentication failures (brute force detection), authorization violations (privilege escalation attempts), unusual access patterns (data exfiltration), API abuse (rate limiting violations), configuration changes (infrastructure drift), network anomalies (unusual traffic patterns), and dependency vulnerabilities (CVE alerts). Use SIEM tools (Splunk, ELK, Sentinel) to correlate security events.

interviewQuestions.q: What is a SIEM and how does it help?
interviewQuestions.a: SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) collects, correlates, and analyzes security events from across your infrastructure. It provides: real-time threat detection, event correlation (linking related security events), compliance reporting, incident investigation, and automated response (SOAR). Popular tools: Splunk, Elastic SIEM, Azure Sentinel, AWS Security Hub.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you implement defense in depth for monitoring?
interviewQuestions.a: Monitor at every layer: network (firewall logs, flow logs), infrastructure (OS events, container events), application (auth logs, API access), and data (database audit logs, file access). Correlate events across layers to detect sophisticated attacks. Use the principle of least privilege and alert on any privilege escalation or unusual access patterns.

tips: Alert on authentication failures exceeding a threshold — this catches brute-force attacks early.
Log all authentication and authorization events — they are essential for incident investigation and compliance.

topics.title: Usage Monitoring
topics.description: Usage monitoring tracks how users interact with the system — feature adoption, user behavior, traffic patterns, and resource consumption. It informs product decisions and capacity planning.

interviewQuestions.q: What does usage monitoring track?
interviewQuestions.a: Feature usage (which features are popular, which are unused), user behavior flows (onboarding completion, checkout funnels), traffic patterns (peak hours, seasonal trends), resource consumption per tenant (for capacity planning and billing), error rates per feature, and A/B test metrics. Tools: Amplitude, Mixpanel, Google Analytics, custom metrics.

interviewQuestions.q: How does usage monitoring inform capacity planning?
interviewQuestions.a: Track growth trends in: requests per second, database size, storage consumption, active users, and peak-to-average ratios. Project future needs based on growth rate. Set up alerts at 70% capacity (time to plan scaling) and 85% (urgent). Correlate usage patterns with infrastructure metrics to predict when you need to scale.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the difference between monitoring and observability?
interviewQuestions.a: Monitoring tells you when something is wrong (predefined metrics and alerts). Observability tells you why something is wrong (ability to ask new questions about your system without deploying new code). Monitoring uses metrics and dashboards. Observability uses metrics, logs, traces, and the ability to explore/correlate them dynamically.

tips: Track feature adoption rates — unused features add maintenance burden and should be considered for removal.
Set capacity alerts at 70% utilization — this gives you time to scale before hitting limits.

topics.title: Instrumentation
topics.description: Instrumentation is the process of adding code to your application to emit metrics, logs, and traces. Good instrumentation is the foundation of observability.

interviewQuestions.q: What should you instrument in your application?
interviewQuestions.a: Request/response metrics (latency, status code, endpoint), business metrics (orders placed, signups, revenue), infrastructure metrics (CPU, memory, disk, connections), dependency calls (database queries, cache operations, API calls), errors and exceptions (with stack traces and context), and custom events (feature usage, user actions).

interviewQuestions.q: What is OpenTelemetry?
interviewQuestions.a: OpenTelemetry is an open-source observability framework that provides APIs and SDKs for generating, collecting, and exporting telemetry data (metrics, logs, traces). It is vendor-neutral — export data to any backend (Datadog, Prometheus, Jaeger, etc.). It standardizes instrumentation so you instrument once and can switch observability backends without code changes.

interviewQuestions.q: What is structured logging and why is it important?
interviewQuestions.a: Structured logging outputs log entries as structured data (JSON) with consistent fields rather than free-form text. Benefits: machine-parseable (easy to query and filter), consistent format, supports correlation IDs for distributed tracing, enables automated analysis, and works well with log aggregation tools (ELK, Datadog). Example: {"level": "error", "msg": "payment failed", "userId": "123", "amount": 50.00, "traceId": "abc"}

tips: Use OpenTelemetry for instrumentation — it is the industry standard and avoids vendor lock-in.
Always include correlation IDs (trace IDs) in logs to enable tracing a request across all services.

topics.title: Visualization & Alerts
topics.description: Dashboards visualize system metrics and alerts notify teams when metrics exceed thresholds. Together they provide real-time visibility and enable rapid incident response.

interviewQuestions.q: What makes a good monitoring dashboard?
interviewQuestions.a: Show the four golden signals prominently (latency, traffic, errors, saturation). Use time-series graphs for trends. Group related metrics logically. Include both current values and historical comparisons. Use color coding (green/yellow/red) for quick status assessment. Keep it focused — a dashboard with 50 graphs is useless. Create separate dashboards for different audiences (SRE, developers, executives).

interviewQuestions.q: What are the best practices for alerting?
interviewQuestions.a: Alert on symptoms (error rate high) not causes (CPU high). Use severity levels (page for critical, notify for warning). Implement alert deduplication and grouping. Set appropriate thresholds to minimize false positives. Include runbooks with every alert. Route alerts to the right team. Use escalation policies. Review and tune alerts regularly — alert fatigue kills response quality.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the monitoring stack?
interviewQuestions.a: Common stack: Prometheus (metrics collection and storage), Grafana (visualization and dashboards), Alertmanager (alert routing and deduplication), Jaeger/Tempo (distributed tracing), Loki/ELK (log aggregation), PagerDuty/OpsGenie (incident management). Cloud alternatives: CloudWatch (AWS), Azure Monitor, Google Cloud Operations Suite, Datadog (SaaS).

tips: Every alert should have a runbook that describes what the alert means and how to respond.
If an alert fires and nobody acts on it, either fix the alert or delete it — unactionable alerts create alert fatigue.

topics.title: Async Request-Reply
topics.description: Decouple backend processing from a frontend host by using an intermediate response and status polling. The client sends a request, gets an immediate acknowledgment with a status URL, and polls for the result.

interviewQuestions.q: How does the Async Request-Reply pattern work?
interviewQuestions.a: 1) Client sends request, 2) Server returns 202 Accepted with a status/polling URL, 3) Client polls the status URL, 4) When processing completes, the status URL returns 200 with the result or a redirect to the result. This decouples long-running backend work from the synchronous HTTP request.

interviewQuestions.q: When should you use this pattern?
interviewQuestions.a: For operations that take seconds to minutes to complete — report generation, video processing, data exports, ML inference. Avoid for operations under 1 second (just make them synchronous) or very long operations over hours (use webhooks/events instead of polling).

interviewQuestions.q: What are alternatives to polling in async request-reply?
interviewQuestions.a: Webhooks (server calls back when done), WebSocket/SSE (server pushes notification), and hybrid (poll initially, switch to push via WebSocket). Polling is simplest and works everywhere but is less efficient than push-based approaches.

tips: Return a Retry-After header with 202 responses to tell clients how long to wait between polls.
Include a progress percentage in the status response for better UX.

topics.title: Claim Check
topics.description: Split a large message into a claim check and a payload. Store the payload externally and send only the claim check (reference) through the message channel to reduce message size.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the Claim Check pattern?
interviewQuestions.a: When messages are too large for a message broker (queue size limits, performance), store the payload in external storage (S3, Blob Storage) and send only a reference (claim check) through the queue. The consumer retrieves the payload using the claim check. This keeps message queues lightweight and fast.

interviewQuestions.q: When should you use the Claim Check pattern?
interviewQuestions.a: When message payload exceeds broker limits (SQS: 256KB, Kafka default: 1MB), when large payloads cause performance issues, when only some consumers need the full payload, or when you want to reduce network bandwidth between services.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the trade-offs?
interviewQuestions.a: Benefits: smaller messages, faster queue throughput, broker does not bottleneck on large payloads. Drawbacks: additional storage dependency, extra latency for retrieval, need to manage payload lifecycle (cleanup/TTL), and complexity of correlating claim checks with payloads.

tips: Use S3/Blob Storage for payloads and pass the object key as the claim check in the message.
Set TTL on stored payloads to avoid storage bloat from unclaimed messages.

topics.title: Choreography
topics.description: Each service in a workflow reacts to events independently without a central orchestrator. Services publish events and other services subscribe and react, creating a decentralized workflow.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the Choreography pattern?
interviewQuestions.a: Services communicate through events without a central controller. Service A publishes an event, Service B reacts and publishes its own event, Service C reacts to that, etc. No single service knows the full workflow — each just reacts to relevant events. This creates loose coupling and independent scalability.

interviewQuestions.q: Choreography vs Orchestration — when to use which?
interviewQuestions.a: Choreography: simple workflows, few services, when loose coupling is priority, and when each service can act independently. Orchestration: complex workflows with many steps, when you need visibility into workflow state, error handling with compensating transactions, and when the workflow logic changes frequently. Most complex systems use a mix.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the drawbacks of choreography?
interviewQuestions.a: Hard to understand the full workflow (no single place shows the flow), difficult to debug (distributed tracing required), cycle detection is complex, adding new steps requires modifying existing services' event handling, and testing end-to-end flows is challenging.

tips: Use choreography for simple, linear workflows and orchestration for complex, branching workflows.
Always implement correlation IDs to trace a workflow across choreographed services.

topics.title: Competing Consumers
topics.description: Multiple consumers compete to process messages from a shared queue. This enables parallel processing and horizontal scaling of message consumers.

interviewQuestions.q: How does the Competing Consumers pattern work?
interviewQuestions.a: Multiple consumer instances pull messages from the same queue. The message broker ensures each message is delivered to only one consumer (point-to-point). If a consumer fails, the message is re-queued for another consumer. This provides: automatic load balancing, horizontal scalability, and fault tolerance.

interviewQuestions.q: What challenges arise with competing consumers?
interviewQuestions.a: Message ordering is not guaranteed across consumers, duplicate processing may occur (use idempotent consumers), some messages may take longer (head-of-line blocking if using visibility timeout), and poison messages can block consumers (use dead-letter queues). Consumer scaling must be balanced with downstream capacity.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you handle message ordering with competing consumers?
interviewQuestions.a: Use message groups/partition keys (SQS FIFO, Kafka partitions) to ensure ordered processing within a group while allowing parallelism across groups. For example, partition by customer ID so all messages for one customer are processed in order, while different customers are processed in parallel.

tips: Use auto-scaling for consumers based on queue depth — scale up when the queue grows, scale down when empty.
Always implement dead-letter queues for messages that fail processing repeatedly.

topics.title: Pipes and Filters
topics.description: Decompose a complex processing task into a series of discrete, reusable stages (filters) connected by channels (pipes). Each filter performs one transformation and passes the result to the next.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the Pipes and Filters pattern?
interviewQuestions.a: A processing pipeline where data passes through a series of independent filters connected by pipes (queues/streams). Each filter does one thing — validate, transform, enrich, route — and passes results to the next filter. Inspired by Unix pipes. Benefits: reusable filters, independent scaling, easy testing, and flexible pipeline composition.

interviewQuestions.q: When should you use Pipes and Filters?
interviewQuestions.a: Data processing pipelines (ETL), image/video processing (resize → watermark → compress), email processing (spam filter → classify → route), order processing (validate → charge → fulfill → notify), and any multi-step transformation where steps can be independently developed and scaled.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the trade-offs?
interviewQuestions.a: Benefits: composability, independent scaling per filter, fault isolation, reusability. Drawbacks: latency increases with each stage (serialized), overhead of serialization between filters, error handling across stages is complex, and data format changes must be coordinated between connected filters.

tips: Use message queues between filters for fault tolerance and independent scaling.
Design each filter to be stateless and idempotent for easy horizontal scaling.

topics.title: Priority Queue
topics.description: Messages sent to a service are processed in priority order rather than FIFO. Higher-priority requests are handled before lower-priority ones, ensuring critical operations get resources first.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you implement priority queues?
interviewQuestions.a: Option 1: Single queue with priority field (broker supports priority). Option 2: Multiple queues per priority level — consumers read from high-priority queue first. Option 3: Weighted consumers — allocate more consumers to higher-priority queues. Option 2 is most common and gives the most control.

interviewQuestions.q: When should you use priority queues?
interviewQuestions.a: When different requests have different SLAs (paid vs free tier), when critical operations must not wait behind bulk operations, real-time vs batch processing, and when system resources are limited and you need to triage. Example: paid API requests get priority over free-tier requests.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the risks of priority queues?
interviewQuestions.a: Starvation: low-priority messages may never get processed if high-priority messages keep arriving. Mitigate with: aging (increase priority over time), minimum bandwidth allocation for each priority level, and separate processing guarantees per tier.

tips: Use separate queues per priority level rather than a single priority queue — it gives you more control over consumer allocation.
Always protect against starvation of low-priority messages with aging or minimum processing guarantees.

topics.title: Publisher/Subscriber
topics.description: A messaging pattern where publishers send messages to a topic (not directly to subscribers). Multiple subscribers can independently receive and process the same message.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the Publisher/Subscriber pattern?
interviewQuestions.a: Publishers emit events to a topic without knowing who consumes them. Subscribers register interest in topics and receive copies of all messages. Unlike point-to-point queues (one consumer per message), pub/sub delivers messages to all subscribers. This enables event-driven architectures with loose coupling.

interviewQuestions.q: How does Pub/Sub differ from Message Queues?
interviewQuestions.a: Message Queue: one message → one consumer (competing consumers). Pub/Sub: one message → all subscribers get a copy. Use queues for task distribution (work items), pub/sub for event notification (broadcast). Kafka blurs this — it supports both patterns with consumer groups.

interviewQuestions.q: What are common pub/sub implementations?
interviewQuestions.a: Apache Kafka (topics with partitions), AWS SNS (fan-out to SQS, Lambda, HTTP), Google Pub/Sub (managed), Redis Pub/Sub (simple, no persistence), Azure Service Bus Topics, and RabbitMQ Exchanges (fanout, topic, headers). Choose based on: persistence needs, ordering requirements, throughput, and managed vs self-hosted.

tips: Use SNS + SQS fan-out in AWS: SNS publishes to a topic, multiple SQS queues subscribe — each gets a copy for independent processing.
Kafka consumer groups let you combine pub/sub (multiple groups) with competing consumers (within a group).

topics.title: Queue-Based Load Leveling
topics.description: Use a queue as a buffer between a task producer and a consumer service to smooth intermittent heavy loads that can cause the service to fail or time out.

interviewQuestions.q: What is Queue-Based Load Leveling?
interviewQuestions.a: A queue sits between producers and consumers, absorbing traffic spikes. During peak load, messages accumulate in the queue. Consumers process at a steady rate. This prevents the backend from being overwhelmed by sudden traffic bursts. The queue acts as a shock absorber, converting bursty traffic into steady flow.

interviewQuestions.q: When should you use this pattern?
interviewQuestions.a: When your backend cannot handle peak traffic (database write limits, external API rate limits), when traffic is bursty but average throughput is manageable, to protect downstream services from overload, and when it is acceptable to process some requests with a slight delay during peaks.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you size the queue and consumers for load leveling?
interviewQuestions.a: Queue size should handle the maximum expected burst duration times peak rate. Consumer count should handle the sustained average throughput. Auto-scale consumers based on queue depth. Set message TTL to avoid processing stale messages. Monitor queue depth trends for capacity planning.

tips: Queue-based load leveling is one of the simplest and most effective patterns for handling traffic spikes.
Combine with auto-scaling consumers: scale up when queue depth increases, scale down when it decreases.

topics.title: Scheduling Agent Supervisor
topics.description: Coordinate a set of distributed actions as a single operation. If any action fails, the supervisor handles recovery through retries or compensating actions to maintain consistency.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the Scheduler Agent Supervisor pattern?
interviewQuestions.a: Scheduler: orchestrates the workflow steps. Agent: executes individual steps on remote services. Supervisor: monitors for failures and triggers recovery (retry, compensate, or alert). Together they coordinate distributed transactions reliably. This is a formalization of the orchestration-based saga pattern.

interviewQuestions.q: How does this differ from choreography?
interviewQuestions.a: Scheduler Agent Supervisor is centralized orchestration — one component knows and controls the workflow. Choreography is decentralized — each service reacts to events independently. SAS provides better visibility, error handling, and workflow management at the cost of a central coordinator.

interviewQuestions.q: What tools implement this pattern?
interviewQuestions.a: Temporal (recommended for complex workflows), AWS Step Functions, Azure Durable Functions, Apache Airflow (batch), Cadence, and Netflix Conductor. These provide durable execution, automatic retries, timeout handling, and compensating transactions out of the box.

tips: Use Temporal or AWS Step Functions instead of building your own workflow orchestrator — it is much harder than it looks.
Design each agent action to be idempotent so the supervisor can safely retry failed steps.

topics.title: Sequential Convoy
topics.description: Process a set of related messages in a defined order while still allowing parallel processing of unrelated message groups. Maintains ordering guarantees within a logical group.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the Sequential Convoy pattern?
interviewQuestions.a: Process groups of related messages in order while allowing parallel processing across groups. For example: process all messages for Customer A in order, and all messages for Customer B in order, but A and B can be processed in parallel. Implemented using message group IDs, Kafka partition keys, or SQS FIFO message groups.

interviewQuestions.q: How does Kafka implement sequential convoy?
interviewQuestions.a: Kafka partitions messages by key. All messages with the same key go to the same partition. Within a partition, messages are strictly ordered. Different partitions are processed in parallel by different consumer group members. This gives you: ordered processing per key + parallel processing across keys.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the challenges of sequential convoy?
interviewQuestions.a: Hot partitions (one group gets disproportionate traffic), consumer failure blocks all messages in that group, rebalancing can temporarily disrupt ordering, and the number of parallel consumers is limited by the number of partitions/groups.

tips: Choose your partition/group key carefully — it determines both ordering and parallelism boundaries.
In Kafka, set the number of partitions to your expected maximum parallelism.

topics.title: CQRS
topics.description: Command Query Responsibility Segregation separates read and write operations into different models. The write model handles commands (create, update, delete) while the read model is optimized for queries.

interviewQuestions.q: What is CQRS?
interviewQuestions.a: CQRS separates the write model (commands: create, update, delete) from the read model (queries). The write model uses a normalized schema optimized for consistency. The read model uses denormalized views optimized for query performance. Changes from the write model are propagated to the read model asynchronously.

interviewQuestions.q: When should you use CQRS?
interviewQuestions.a: When read and write workloads have very different characteristics (read-heavy with complex queries), when you need different scaling for reads vs writes, when the read model needs a different data shape than the write model, and when combined with Event Sourcing. Avoid for simple CRUD applications — CQRS adds significant complexity.

interviewQuestions.q: How is CQRS typically implemented?
interviewQuestions.a: Write side: domain model with business logic writes to a normalized database. Read side: materialized views/projections optimized for specific queries. Sync mechanism: events, CDC (Change Data Capture), or database triggers propagate changes from write to read side. The read side may use a different database technology (Elasticsearch for search, Redis for fast lookups).

tips: Only use CQRS when read and write models are genuinely different — for simple CRUD, it is over-engineering.
CQRS pairs naturally with Event Sourcing — events from the write side are projected into read models.

topics.title: Event Sourcing
topics.description: Store the state of an entity as a sequence of immutable events rather than the current state. The current state is derived by replaying events. Provides a complete audit trail and temporal queries.

interviewQuestions.q: What is Event Sourcing?
interviewQuestions.a: Instead of storing current state (balance = 100), store all events that led to that state (deposited 200, withdrew 50, deposited 30, withdrew 80). Current state is derived by replaying events. Events are immutable — never modified or deleted. Provides: complete audit trail, temporal queries, event replay for debugging, and natural fit with event-driven architecture.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the challenges of Event Sourcing?
interviewQuestions.a: Event schema evolution over time, potential performance issues with long event streams (use snapshots), eventual consistency between write and read models, increased storage requirements, and steep learning curve. Not suitable for simple CRUD where current state is sufficient.

interviewQuestions.q: How do snapshots work in Event Sourcing?
interviewQuestions.a: Periodically save the current state as a snapshot. To rebuild state, load the latest snapshot then replay only events after it, instead of replaying all events from the beginning. This bounds the replay time regardless of how many historical events exist.

tips: Event Sourcing is ideal for financial systems, audit-critical applications, and systems that need temporal queries.
Always implement snapshots for event streams that grow beyond a few hundred events.

topics.title: Index Table
topics.description: Create indexes over fields in data stores that are frequently referenced by queries. Improves query performance by allowing efficient lookups on non-primary key fields.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the Index Table pattern?
interviewQuestions.a: Create separate lookup tables or secondary indexes that map non-primary key fields to the primary data. In NoSQL databases that only support primary key lookups, this enables efficient queries on other fields. For example, a secondary index table mapping email → userId allows looking up users by email.

interviewQuestions.q: When should you use the Index Table pattern?
interviewQuestions.a: When your data store only supports primary key lookups (DynamoDB, Cassandra), when queries frequently filter on non-primary key fields, and when denormalization for every query pattern is impractical. Also useful in RDBMS when you need composite or covering indexes for complex query patterns.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the trade-offs?
interviewQuestions.a: Benefits: fast lookups on non-primary fields. Drawbacks: additional storage, write amplification (every write must update all index tables), index consistency (eventual consistency if async), and maintenance overhead. In DynamoDB, use Global Secondary Indexes (GSI) instead of manual index tables when possible.

tips: In DynamoDB, use GSIs (Global Secondary Indexes) rather than maintaining separate index tables manually.
Design your indexes around your query patterns — every index should serve at least one critical query path.

topics.title: Materialized View
topics.description: Pre-compute and store query results as a denormalized view. When the source data changes, update the view. This optimizes read-heavy workloads by avoiding expensive joins and aggregations at query time.

interviewQuestions.q: What is a Materialized View?
interviewQuestions.a: A precomputed, stored result of a query that is refreshed periodically or on data change. Unlike a regular view (computed at query time), a materialized view stores the results physically. This trades storage and write complexity for much faster reads — complex joins and aggregations are computed once, not per query.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you keep materialized views up to date?
interviewQuestions.a: Periodic refresh (REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW every N minutes), trigger-based (update on source data change), CDC-based (Change Data Capture streams changes to the view), event-driven (events trigger view updates), and incremental refresh (only update changed portions). The right approach depends on freshness requirements and update frequency.

interviewQuestions.q: When should you use materialized views?
interviewQuestions.a: For dashboards and reporting (precomputed aggregations), search results (denormalized for fast retrieval), CQRS read models, API responses that combine data from multiple tables, and any read-heavy scenario where the source query is expensive. Avoid for data that changes extremely frequently or where real-time accuracy is required.

tips: Materialized views are the simplest form of CQRS — precomputed read models for specific query patterns.
Use REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY in PostgreSQL to avoid locking reads during refresh.

topics.title: Sharding Pattern
topics.description: Divide a data store into horizontal partitions (shards). Each shard has the same schema but holds a different subset of data, enabling horizontal scaling across multiple database servers.

interviewQuestions.q: What sharding strategies exist as a cloud pattern?
interviewQuestions.a: Lookup strategy: mapping table determines which shard holds which data. Range strategy: contiguous ranges of shard keys go to specific shards. Hash strategy: hash function distributes keys evenly across shards. Each strategy has trade-offs in terms of distribution evenness, range query support, and rebalancing ease.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you handle cross-shard queries?
interviewQuestions.a: Fan-out: query all shards and merge results (expensive). Denormalization: duplicate data to avoid cross-shard queries. Global tables: small, frequently joined tables replicated to all shards. Application-level joins: fetch from each shard and join in code. Best approach: design shard keys to minimize cross-shard queries.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you rebalance shards?
interviewQuestions.a: Add new shards and migrate data (resharding), use consistent hashing to minimize data movement, employ virtual shards (many virtual shards mapped to fewer physical nodes — remap virtual to physical when scaling), or use managed services (CockroachDB, Vitess, Citus) that handle rebalancing automatically.

tips: Use consistent hashing with virtual nodes to minimize data movement when adding or removing shards.
Managed sharding solutions (Vitess, Citus, CockroachDB) handle the hardest parts — prefer them over DIY sharding.

topics.title: Static Content Hosting
topics.description: Deploy static content (HTML, CSS, JS, images) to a cloud-based storage service that can deliver them directly to the client, reducing load on compute resources.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the Static Content Hosting pattern?
interviewQuestions.a: Serve static files (HTML, CSS, JS, images, fonts) directly from object storage (S3, Azure Blob, GCS) and CDN, bypassing the application server entirely. The application server only handles dynamic API requests. This reduces compute costs, improves scalability (storage + CDN scale infinitely), and improves performance.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you implement static content hosting?
interviewQuestions.a: Upload static assets to object storage (S3 bucket), place a CDN (CloudFront, Cloudflare) in front for global caching, configure CORS for cross-origin API calls, use versioned filenames for cache busting, and set appropriate cache-control headers. For SPAs, configure the CDN to route all paths to index.html.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the benefits over serving static files from the application server?
interviewQuestions.a: Dramatically reduced server load (no CPU/memory used for static files), infinite scalability of storage + CDN, lower latency (CDN edge locations), lower cost (storage is cheaper than compute), and separation of concerns (frontend deployed independently from backend).

tips: Always serve static assets from object storage + CDN, not from your application server.
Use CI/CD to automatically deploy static assets to S3/Blob Storage on every frontend build.

topics.title: Valet Key
topics.description: Use a token or key that provides clients with restricted direct access to a specific resource, reducing the need for the application to act as a proxy for data transfer.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the Valet Key pattern?
interviewQuestions.a: Generate a time-limited, scoped token that gives clients direct access to a specific resource (e.g., upload a file to S3). The client uses this token to interact directly with the storage service, bypassing the application server. This offloads data transfer from the application, reducing bandwidth and CPU usage.

interviewQuestions.q: How are pre-signed URLs an implementation of this pattern?
interviewQuestions.a: AWS S3 pre-signed URLs are the classic example: the server generates a URL that grants temporary PUT/GET access to a specific S3 object. The client uploads directly to S3 using this URL. The URL expires after a set time. No credentials are exposed — the signature is embedded in the URL.

interviewQuestions.q: When should you use the Valet Key pattern?
interviewQuestions.a: File uploads (images, videos, documents) — avoid proxying large files through the application. File downloads (serving large files directly from storage). Any scenario where the application does not need to process the data stream. This is standard practice for most cloud applications handling file uploads.

tips: Always use pre-signed URLs for file uploads — never proxy large files through your application server.
Set short expiration times (5-15 minutes) on valet keys and scope them to the minimum required permissions.

topics.title: Ambassador
topics.description: Create helper services that send network requests on behalf of a consumer service. An ambassador acts as an out-of-process proxy for handling cross-cutting concerns like retries, circuit breaking, and logging.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the Ambassador pattern?
interviewQuestions.a: A helper proxy that runs alongside a service, handling cross-cutting network concerns: retries with backoff, circuit breaking, TLS termination, logging, monitoring, and authentication. The service communicates with localhost; the ambassador handles the complexity of external communication. Envoy proxy in Istio service mesh is the canonical example.

interviewQuestions.q: How does Ambassador differ from Sidecar?
interviewQuestions.a: Ambassador specifically handles outbound network communication on behalf of the service. Sidecar is a broader pattern — any helper process running alongside the main service. Ambassador is a specialized type of sidecar. All ambassadors are sidecars, but not all sidecars are ambassadors.

interviewQuestions.q: When should you use the Ambassador pattern?
interviewQuestions.a: When multiple services need the same networking features (retries, circuit breaking, mTLS), when you want to add resilience without modifying application code, for polyglot environments (different languages share the same ambassador proxy), and when adopting a service mesh incrementally.

tips: Envoy proxy (used in Istio, Consul Connect) is the most popular ambassador implementation.
Use ambassadors to add resilience features without modifying your application code.

topics.title: Anti-Corruption Layer
topics.description: Place a facade or adapter layer between new and legacy systems to prevent the legacy system's model from corrupting the new system's domain model.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the Anti-Corruption Layer?
interviewQuestions.a: A translation layer between two systems (typically new and legacy) that converts data and calls between their different models. It prevents the legacy system's outdated concepts, naming, and data structures from leaking into the new system. The ACL translates, maps, and validates data in both directions.

interviewQuestions.q: When should you use an Anti-Corruption Layer?
interviewQuestions.a: When integrating with legacy systems that have different domain models, during incremental migration (Strangler Fig), when communicating with external APIs that have different data formats, and when bounded contexts in DDD need to communicate without contaminating each other's models.

interviewQuestions.q: How does ACL relate to the Strangler Fig pattern?
interviewQuestions.a: They are often used together. Strangler Fig incrementally replaces a legacy system by routing requests to new services. The ACL sits between old and new, translating data models so both can coexist during the migration. As migration completes, the ACL is removed.

tips: The ACL should be thin — translate data, not implement business logic.
Pair Anti-Corruption Layer with Strangler Fig for incremental legacy migration.

topics.title: Backends for Frontends (BFF)
topics.description: Create separate backend services for each frontend (web, mobile, IoT). Each BFF is tailored to the specific needs and constraints of its frontend client.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the Backend for Frontend pattern?
interviewQuestions.a: Instead of one general-purpose API for all clients, create separate backend services tailored to each frontend. A mobile BFF returns minimal data (bandwidth-efficient), a web BFF returns richer data for desktop screens, and an IoT BFF handles device-specific protocols. Each BFF aggregates, transforms, and optimizes data for its specific client.

interviewQuestions.q: When should you use BFF over a single API?
interviewQuestions.a: When different frontends have very different data needs, when mobile needs smaller payloads than web, when you want frontend teams to own their backend (team autonomy), when different clients require different authentication mechanisms, and when performance requirements differ significantly across clients.

interviewQuestions.q: How does BFF relate to GraphQL?
interviewQuestions.a: GraphQL can reduce the need for BFF because clients query exactly what they need. However, BFF is still useful when: client-specific business logic is needed, you want to aggregate from multiple microservices, or clients have fundamentally different interaction patterns beyond just data selection.

tips: BFF gives frontend teams ownership of their backend — each team can optimize independently.
GraphQL can be a simpler alternative to BFF when the main issue is over-fetching/under-fetching.

topics.title: Compute Resource Consolidation
topics.description: Consolidate multiple tasks or operations into a single computational unit to improve resource utilization and reduce costs. Group related workloads onto shared infrastructure.

interviewQuestions.q: What is Compute Resource Consolidation?
interviewQuestions.a: Running multiple tasks, services, or workloads on shared compute resources instead of dedicated instances. Examples: multiple microservices in one Kubernetes pod, multiple functions in one container, or multiple lightweight services on one VM. This improves utilization and reduces cost at the expense of isolation.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the trade-offs?
interviewQuestions.a: Benefits: better resource utilization, lower cost, simpler management of fewer resources. Drawbacks: reduced isolation (noisy neighbor), shared failure domain, harder to scale individual components, and security boundaries are weaker. Use for related, similarly-scaled services; keep critical services isolated.

tips: Understand when to apply Compute Resource Consolidation vs alternatives in system design interviews.
Consider the trade-offs before adopting this pattern.

topics.title: External Configuration Store
topics.description: Move configuration out of the application deployment package into a centralized external store. Enables runtime configuration changes without redeployment.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the External Configuration Store pattern?
interviewQuestions.a: Store application configuration in a centralized, external service (Consul, etcd, AWS Parameter Store, Azure App Configuration) rather than in config files or environment variables. Benefits: change config without redeployment, share config across services, audit trail, encryption for secrets, and feature flags.

interviewQuestions.q: What tools implement this pattern?
interviewQuestions.a: HashiCorp Consul/Vault, AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store, AWS AppConfig, Azure App Configuration, etcd (Kubernetes), Spring Cloud Config, and LaunchDarkly (feature flags). For secrets specifically: AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, HashiCorp Vault.

tips: Understand when to apply External Configuration Store vs alternatives in system design interviews.
Consider the trade-offs before adopting this pattern.

topics.title: Gateway Aggregation
topics.description: Use a gateway to aggregate multiple individual requests into a single request. This reduces chattiness between client and backend services.

interviewQuestions.q: What is Gateway Aggregation?
interviewQuestions.a: The API Gateway combines multiple backend service calls into a single client request. Instead of the client making 5 API calls to load a page, it makes 1 call to the gateway which fans out to 5 services, combines the results, and returns a single response. This reduces network round trips and improves client performance.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the trade-offs?
interviewQuestions.a: Benefits: fewer client-server round trips, simpler client logic, reduced latency for aggregated requests. Drawbacks: gateway becomes a bottleneck, increased gateway complexity, partial failure handling (what if one backend service fails?), and harder to cache individual responses.

tips: Understand when to apply Gateway Aggregation vs alternatives in system design interviews.
Consider the trade-offs before adopting this pattern.

topics.title: Gateway Offloading
topics.description: Offload shared or specialized functionality from individual services to a gateway proxy. Common for SSL termination, authentication, rate limiting, and logging.

interviewQuestions.q: What is Gateway Offloading?
interviewQuestions.a: Move cross-cutting concerns from individual services to the API gateway: SSL/TLS termination, authentication/authorization, rate limiting, IP whitelisting, compression, caching, CORS handling, and request/response logging. Services focus on business logic; the gateway handles infrastructure concerns.

interviewQuestions.q: What are common offloading scenarios?
interviewQuestions.a: SSL termination (decrypt at gateway, plain HTTP internally), authentication (validate JWT at gateway, pass claims downstream), rate limiting (per-client throttling), response compression (gzip at gateway), request logging/tracing (add correlation IDs), and static response caching.

tips: Understand when to apply Gateway Offloading vs alternatives in system design interviews.
Consider the trade-offs before adopting this pattern.

topics.title: Gateway Routing
topics.description: Route requests to multiple services using a single endpoint. The gateway routes based on URL path, headers, or other request attributes, acting as a reverse proxy.

interviewQuestions.q: What is Gateway Routing?
interviewQuestions.a: The gateway acts as a reverse proxy, routing client requests to different backend services based on the request. /api/users → User Service, /api/orders → Order Service, /api/products → Product Service. Clients interact with a single endpoint; the gateway handles routing. This simplifies the client and allows backend service reorganization without affecting clients.

interviewQuestions.q: What routing strategies exist?
interviewQuestions.a: Path-based (/api/v1/users → Service A), header-based (X-Version: 2 → Service B), query parameter-based, cookie-based (canary deployments), weighted (10% to new version), and geographic (route based on client location).

tips: Understand when to apply Gateway Routing vs alternatives in system design interviews.
Consider the trade-offs before adopting this pattern.

topics.title: Leader Election
topics.description: Coordinate distributed instances by electing one as the leader that manages the workload. If the leader fails, a new one is elected. Essential for tasks that must be performed by exactly one instance.

interviewQuestions.q: What is Leader Election?
interviewQuestions.a: In a cluster of identical instances, one is elected as leader to coordinate work, manage shared resources, or perform singleton tasks (cron jobs, data migration). If the leader fails, remaining instances elect a new leader. Implemented using consensus algorithms (Raft, Paxos), distributed locks (Redis, Zookeeper), or cloud services (DynamoDB conditional writes).

interviewQuestions.q: When do you need leader election?
interviewQuestions.a: Scheduled tasks that must run exactly once (not on every instance), write operations to shared resources, coordination of distributed workflows, partition assignment in stream processing (Kafka consumer groups), and master node selection in database clusters.

interviewQuestions.q: What tools provide leader election?
interviewQuestions.a: Zookeeper, etcd (Kubernetes uses it), Consul, Redis (Redlock), DynamoDB (conditional writes), and cloud-native: AWS ECS task placement, Kubernetes leader-for-life pattern. For most applications, use a managed service rather than implementing your own consensus algorithm.

tips: Understand when to apply Leader Election vs alternatives in system design interviews.
Consider the trade-offs before adopting this pattern.

topics.title: Sidecar
topics.description: Deploy components of an application into a separate process or container alongside the main service. The sidecar shares the same lifecycle and provides supporting functionality.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the Sidecar pattern?
interviewQuestions.a: A helper container/process that runs alongside the main service container in the same pod/host. The sidecar extends the main service with additional capabilities: logging, monitoring, proxy (Envoy), configuration management, TLS termination, and service mesh functionality. Both share the same network namespace and can communicate via localhost.

interviewQuestions.q: What are common sidecar use cases?
interviewQuestions.a: Service mesh proxy (Envoy in Istio), log collection (Fluentd sidecar), monitoring agent (Datadog agent), configuration sync (Consul agent), TLS certificate management, and database proxy (Cloud SQL Proxy for GCP). The sidecar adds functionality without modifying the main service code.

interviewQuestions.q: How does the sidecar pattern relate to service mesh?
interviewQuestions.a: Service meshes like Istio deploy an Envoy sidecar proxy alongside every service. The sidecar intercepts all network traffic, providing: mTLS, retries, circuit breaking, load balancing, observability, and traffic management. The application is unaware of the sidecar — it just communicates via localhost.

tips: Understand when to apply Sidecar vs alternatives in system design interviews.
Consider the trade-offs before adopting this pattern.

topics.title: Strangler Fig
topics.description: Incrementally migrate a legacy system by gradually replacing specific pieces of functionality with new applications and services. Over time, the new system replaces all features of the old system.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the Strangler Fig pattern?
interviewQuestions.a: Named after strangler fig vines that gradually envelop a host tree. Route specific requests to new services while the old system handles everything else. Over time, more functionality moves to new services. Eventually the old system is fully replaced and can be decommissioned. This minimizes risk compared to a full rewrite.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you implement the Strangler Fig pattern?
interviewQuestions.a: 1) Identify a bounded context to extract, 2) Build the new service, 3) Set up a routing layer (API gateway/reverse proxy), 4) Route requests for that context to the new service, 5) Verify correctness, 6) Repeat for next context. Use an Anti-Corruption Layer between old and new systems to translate data models.

interviewQuestions.q: Why is Strangler Fig preferred over a full rewrite?
interviewQuestions.a: Full rewrites are risky: they take longer than expected, require feature parity before launch, and the old system keeps evolving. Strangler Fig delivers incremental value, reduces risk (each step is small and reversible), allows the team to learn gradually, and the old system continues serving users throughout the migration.

tips: Start with the most independent, well-bounded functionality for the first extraction.
Use an API gateway or reverse proxy as the routing layer between old and new systems.

topics.title: Deployment Stamps
topics.description: Deploy multiple independent copies (stamps) of application components, including data stores. Each stamp serves a subset of tenants. Provides isolation, scalability, and geographic distribution.

interviewQuestions.q: What are Deployment Stamps?
interviewQuestions.a: Independent, identical copies of your entire application stack (compute + storage) that each serve a subset of tenants. Each stamp is fully self-contained. Benefits: tenant isolation (noisy neighbor prevention), independent scaling per stamp, geographic distribution, and easier compliance (data residency). Used by large SaaS providers.

interviewQuestions.q: When should you use Deployment Stamps?
interviewQuestions.a: When you need strong tenant isolation, when different tenants have different geographic requirements (data residency), when you need to scale beyond the limits of a single deployment, and when you want to limit the blast radius of failures to a subset of tenants.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you route tenants to stamps?
interviewQuestions.a: A global routing layer maps tenants to stamps. Options: DNS-based routing (tenant1.app.com → stamp-A), API gateway lookup (tenant header → stamp routing), database lookup table, and DNS + load balancer combination. The routing layer is the only shared component.

tips: Deployment Stamps are ideal for multi-tenant SaaS — each stamp provides isolation and independent scaling.
Keep the routing layer simple and highly available — it is the single point of failure across all stamps.

topics.title: Geodes
topics.description: Deploy backend services into a set of geographical nodes, each of which can service any client request from any region. Also known as geographically distributed deployments.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the Geode pattern?
interviewQuestions.a: Deploy identical service instances across multiple geographic regions. Any instance can handle any request. Data is replicated across regions. Traffic is routed to the nearest healthy region. Unlike Deployment Stamps (which partition tenants), Geodes serve all users from any region. This minimizes latency for global users and provides regional failover.

interviewQuestions.q: How does Geode differ from Deployment Stamps?
interviewQuestions.a: Geodes: any node can serve any user, data is replicated globally, focused on latency reduction and availability. Deployment Stamps: each stamp serves specific tenants, data is partitioned per stamp, focused on isolation and scaling. Geodes are for globally distributed users; Stamps are for multi-tenant isolation.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the challenges of the Geode pattern?
interviewQuestions.a: Data replication across regions (consistency vs latency trade-off), conflict resolution for concurrent writes in different regions, increased infrastructure cost, complex deployment pipelines, and compliance with data residency regulations (some data cannot leave certain regions).

tips: Use Geodes when you need low latency for globally distributed users — not just for availability.
Combine with eventually consistent data stores (DynamoDB Global Tables, Cosmos DB) for seamless global replication.

topics.title: Health Endpoint Monitoring
topics.description: Implement functional checks in an application that external tools and services can access through exposed endpoints at regular intervals. Detect operational issues before they impact users.

interviewQuestions.q: What should health endpoints check?
interviewQuestions.a: Shallow health: is the process alive (liveness probe). Deep health: can the service function correctly — database connectivity, cache availability, external API reachability, disk space, memory. Return degraded status if non-critical dependencies are down. Include dependency status in the response for debugging.

interviewQuestions.q: How do health endpoints integrate with infrastructure?
interviewQuestions.a: Load balancers: remove unhealthy instances from the pool. Kubernetes: liveness (restart if unhealthy), readiness (remove from service if not ready), startup (wait for initialization). Monitoring: trigger alerts when health changes. Auto-scaling: replace unhealthy instances. DNS failover: redirect traffic from unhealthy regions.

interviewQuestions.q: What are best practices for health endpoints?
interviewQuestions.a: Separate liveness and readiness endpoints, do not expose sensitive information, set appropriate timeouts (health check should not hang), cache health check results briefly (avoid hammering dependencies), and include version and uptime info for debugging.

tips: Implement separate /health/live (is the process running?) and /health/ready (can it serve traffic?) endpoints.
Health check responses should be fast — cache dependency checks for a few seconds to avoid cascading load.

topics.title: Throttling
topics.description: Control the consumption of resources by limiting the rate of requests. Throttling protects services from being overwhelmed and ensures fair resource allocation across consumers.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the common rate limiting algorithms?
interviewQuestions.a: Token Bucket: tokens added at fixed rate, each request consumes a token, allows bursts. Leaky Bucket: requests processed at fixed rate, excess queued. Fixed Window: count requests per time window (e.g., 100/minute). Sliding Window: smoothed version of fixed window to avoid burst at window boundaries. Token Bucket is the most popular for its simplicity and burst allowance.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you implement distributed rate limiting?
interviewQuestions.a: Centralized counter (Redis INCR with TTL), sliding window in Redis (sorted sets), distributed token bucket, or API gateway built-in rate limiting (Kong, AWS API Gateway). Redis is the most common backing store for distributed rate limiting due to atomic operations and speed.

interviewQuestions.q: What should happen when a request is throttled?
interviewQuestions.a: Return HTTP 429 Too Many Requests with Retry-After header (how long to wait). Include rate limit headers: X-RateLimit-Limit (max requests), X-RateLimit-Remaining (remaining), X-RateLimit-Reset (when window resets). Log throttled requests for monitoring. Consider graceful degradation instead of hard rejection for internal services.

tips: Always return HTTP 429 with Retry-After and rate limit headers — it helps clients implement proper backoff.
Implement rate limiting at the API gateway level for simplicity and consistency across all services.

project.title: Build a Resilient Cloud-Native Microservice
project.description: Design and implement a production-ready microservice that employs multiple cloud design patterns: retry, circuit breaker, bulkhead, sidecar, strangler fig, and CQRS. This is the culmination of everything you've learned — a service that's built to survive in production.
objectives: Implement retry, circuit breaker, and bulkhead resilience patterns
Apply CQRS to separate read and write models for performance
Use the sidecar pattern for cross-cutting concerns (logging, metrics, auth)
Implement the ambassador pattern for external service communication
Design for graceful degradation when downstream services fail

tasks.title: Build the core microservice
tasks.description: A product catalog service with CRUD, search, and inventory tracking.

tasks.title: Add retry + circuit breaker
tasks.description: Wrap all external calls with retry (3 attempts, exponential backoff) and circuit breaker.

tasks.title: Implement bulkhead pattern
tasks.description: Isolate thread pools for different downstream services. A slow service shouldn't affect others.

tasks.title: Apply CQRS
tasks.description: Separate write model (PostgreSQL) from read model (Elasticsearch). Sync via events.

tasks.title: Implement sidecar pattern
tasks.description: Deploy a sidecar container that handles logging, metrics, and health checks for the main service.

tasks.title: Add graceful degradation
tasks.description: When the recommendation service is down, serve cached recommendations. When search is down, return popular items.

tasks.title: Implement strangler fig migration
tasks.description: Demonstrate incremental migration: route 20% of traffic to new service, 80% to legacy.

architectureHints: Circuit breaker states: CLOSED (normal) → OPEN (failing fast) → HALF-OPEN (testing recovery). Track error rate over sliding window.
Bulkhead: use separate thread/connection pools for each downstream service to prevent cascade failures
CQRS allows independent scaling of reads (Elasticsearch cluster) and writes (PostgreSQL with replicas)
The sidecar runs as a separate container in the same pod/docker-compose service — shares network namespace

capstoneProject.title: MegaScale: Distributed Social Media Platform
capstoneProject.description: Design and build a distributed social media platform from scratch that demonstrates mastery of ALL system design concepts. This 6-phase capstone covers everything from basic APIs to multi-region deployment, making it the ultimate portfolio project for top product-based company interviews at Google, Meta, Amazon, and Netflix.
objectives: Design a complete social media architecture handling 10M+ users
Implement a news feed with fan-out-on-write for real-time delivery
Build real-time messaging with WebSockets across distributed servers
Handle media uploads with CDN integration and image processing
Implement full-text search with autocomplete and relevance ranking
Deploy with horizontal scaling, database sharding, and multi-region failover
Set up comprehensive observability: metrics, logs, traces, and SLO dashboards
Apply chaos engineering to validate system resilience

tasks.title: Phase 1: Design the system architecture
tasks.description: Create a high-level architecture diagram covering all services, databases, queues, and CDN. Define API contracts between services.

tasks.title: Phase 1: Build User Service
tasks.description: User registration, authentication (JWT), profiles, and follow/unfollow with PostgreSQL.

tasks.title: Phase 1: Add rate limiting and API gateway
tasks.description: Token bucket rate limiter per user. API gateway routes requests to microservices.

tasks.title: Phase 1: Set up Redis caching layer
tasks.description: Cache user profiles, session data, and hot configurations. Implement cache-aside pattern.

tasks.title: Phase 2: Build Post Service
tasks.description: Create/read/delete posts with text, images, and links. Store posts in PostgreSQL, media metadata in S3.

tasks.title: Phase 2: Implement media upload pipeline
tasks.description: Upload images to S3/MinIO, generate thumbnails asynchronously, serve via CDN with proper cache headers.

tasks.title: Phase 2: Build the News Feed
tasks.description: Fan-out-on-write: when a user posts, push to all followers' feed caches in Redis. Paginate with cursor-based scrolling.

tasks.title: Phase 2: Add like/comment system
tasks.description: Like counts with Redis counters for speed, persisted to PostgreSQL asynchronously. Nested comment threads.

tasks.title: Phase 3: Build Messaging Service
tasks.description: Real-time 1:1 and group chat using WebSockets. Message persistence in PostgreSQL. Redis pub/sub for cross-server fanout.

tasks.title: Phase 3: Implement Notification Service
tasks.description: Push notifications for likes, comments, follows, and messages. Event-driven with RabbitMQ/Kafka.

tasks.title: Phase 3: Set up event bus
tasks.description: Kafka or RabbitMQ event bus connecting all services. Define event schemas and consumer groups.

tasks.title: Phase 4: Build Search Service
tasks.description: Full-text search on posts and users using Elasticsearch. Support autocomplete, filters, and relevance ranking.

tasks.title: Phase 4: Implement Recommendation Engine
tasks.description: Suggest users to follow based on mutual connections and content similarity. Use collaborative filtering.

tasks.title: Phase 4: Add trending topics
tasks.description: Track hashtag frequency with sliding window counters in Redis. Display top trending topics.

tasks.title: Phase 5: Implement database sharding
tasks.description: Shard user data by user_id hash across multiple PostgreSQL instances. Implement shard routing layer.

tasks.title: Phase 5: Add horizontal scaling
tasks.description: Run each service with 3+ replicas behind load balancers. Verify stateless design works.

tasks.title: Phase 5: Implement circuit breakers
tasks.description: Add circuit breakers on all inter-service calls. Define fallback behavior for each service.

tasks.title: Phase 5: Add multi-region support
tasks.description: Deploy read replicas in a second region. Route users to nearest region. Handle cross-region replication lag.

tasks.title: Phase 6: Set up Prometheus + Grafana
tasks.description: Instrument all services with RED metrics. Build service health dashboards with meaningful alerts.

tasks.title: Phase 6: Implement distributed tracing
tasks.description: Add OpenTelemetry tracing across all services. Track request flow from API gateway to database.

tasks.title: Phase 6: Build SLO dashboard
tasks.description: Define SLOs for availability (99.9%), latency (p99 < 500ms), and error rate (< 0.1%). Track error budgets.

tasks.title: Phase 6: Chaos engineering
tasks.description: Kill random services, inject latency, exhaust database connections. Verify graceful degradation.

tasks.title: Bonus: Implement content moderation pipeline
tasks.description: Async content moderation with ML-based toxicity detection. Flag and queue for review.

tasks.title: Bonus: Add A/B testing framework
tasks.description: Feature flag service with percentage-based rollouts. Track metrics per experiment.

tasks.title: Bonus: Write the System Design Document
tasks.description: Document the complete architecture: trade-offs, capacity planning, failure modes, and runbooks. This is your interview portfolio piece.

architectureHints: Start with a monolith per bounded context (User, Post, Feed, Chat), then split into microservices as complexity grows
Fan-out-on-write for the news feed: when a user posts, push the post ID to each follower's Redis sorted set. For celebrity users (>10K followers), use fan-out-on-read to avoid hot keys.
Use Kafka for the event bus — its durability and replay capability are essential for a system this complex
Shard PostgreSQL by user_id using hash-based partitioning. Keep a routing table in the API gateway.
For real-time messaging, each WebSocket server subscribes to Redis pub/sub channels per conversation. Load balance WebSocket connections with sticky sessions.
Media pipeline: client uploads to pre-signed S3 URL -> S3 event triggers Lambda/worker -> generate thumbnails -> update CDN -> notify client via WebSocket
Elasticsearch should be a secondary index, not a primary data store. Sync from PostgreSQL via CDC (Debezium) or application-level events.
Design for failure: every service should have a fallback. Feed down? Show cached version. Search down? Show trending posts. Chat down? Queue messages for later delivery.
